1:1.text: Wisdom. In this book, Wisdom is taken for the Deity, the Son, or the gift communicated to men, Proverbs 3:19., and Wisdom 7:25.

1:3.text: God. This is eternal and unsearchable, more than the sand of the sea, etc., Isaias 40:12.

1:4.text: Created. Or "generated," if it be understood of the Son.
The wisdom which is given to man, was in God before the creation, Proverbs 8:22.
The decree regarding the incarnation was from eternity.

1:5.text: Commandments. The wise will observe the law (Deuteronomy 4:6.; Haydock) and the Scriptures. This verse is not in the Greek of Rome, etc., but it is in the edition of Complutensian and Camerarius.

1:9.text: In the Holy Ghost, or from himself. See Wisdom 1:5., and 7:22. Greek omits these words.

1:11.text: Joy. Eternal glory is the fruit of the fear of the Lord; not that this virtue sufficeth, but it is the beginning, grounded on true faith, and bringeth forth other virtues, and fruits of the Holy Ghost, and a joyful crown in the end.
The virtuous enjoy or deserve honour.

1:16.text: They are free from evil dispositions, Wisdom 8:19., and Job 31:18.
Greek, "it shall be intrusted to their seed."
"His mercy is fixed with their seed."
This includes both men and women.

1:17.text: Religiousness, or proper application of knowledge.
A learned impious man is most dangerous. This and the two next verses are not in Greek.

1:27.text: Sin, by vigilance or by repentance.

1:29.text: To him, as may be seen in the lives of the patriarchs.

1:32.text: Sinner. Such imagine that God's service is insupportable.

1:35.text: The meek shall possess the land, Matthew 5:4.

1:39.text: Down. Pride is usually thus treated, Matthew 23:12.

2:1.text: God. All must be tried, Tobias 12:13., and Hebrews 12:6., and Luke 24:46. But God will grant sufficient grace, 1 Corinthians 10:13.

2:2.text: Humble. Greek, "regulate thy heart, and endure, and make not haste in the time of trouble," (Haydock) inflicted by God.
Desire not death, in order to be covered from the miseries of life; for that would (Worthington) border on despair.

2:3.text: Patience. Or expectation of being relieved.

2:5.text: Humiliation. It shews what they really are.

2:10.text: This verse is not in Greek. To avoid such frequent repetitions, it would be well to mark the passages in different characters, (Haydock) or between crotchets, as in the French version.
Yet the Greek editions are not uniform. See Drusius and Haeschel.

2:14.text: Ways. Attempting to reconcile the service of the world with that of God, who rejects hypocrites, (Haydock) the inconstant, and faint-hearted, ver. 15., and 3 Kings 18:21.
Virtues and sins will receive their due.

2:20.text: Hearts. With God's grace (Haydock) to fight manfully.
Sanctify. Greek, "humble their souls, saying, we shall," etc., ver. 22.
Prayer, vigilance, and humility are the best dispositions to resist the devil.

2:22.text: Men. He alludes to 2 Kings 24:14. In this world, God punishes with mercy; in the next, with severity, Daniel 13:23.

3:1.text: Love. The progeny of God's children brings forth the fruits of obedience and love.
This verse is not in Greek.

3:3.text: Seeking. Greek, "and hath confirmed the judgment," etc.
God will revenge any disrespect shewn to parents.

3:7.text: Father. Greek, "Lord....mother (8.) and will serve them," etc.

3:7.src: St. Ambrose in Luke ii., and xviii.; Exodus 20:12., and Job 4:3.
3:7.text: Though you may feed your parents, you are still much in their debt.

3:9.text: Grabe marks this in another character, and substitutes "them" for him, ver. 10.
And his, etc., is wholly omitted.

3:11.text: Foundations. St. Augustine (City of God 22:8.) gives a memorable instance. The pagans deemed such curses most terrible.
They are executed upon the wicked.

3:12.text: Thee. Boast not of thy superior talents, nor say any thing disrespectful.

3:13.text: A father. Greek, "mother." Alexander [the Great] was ashamed of having Philip for his father, pretending that he was the son of Jupiter Ammon. His mother Olympias, with much ingenuity, wrote to him, that he would thus make Juno her powerful rival!

3:15.text: Fail. Bis pueri senes. They do not lose the character of fathers, how infirm soever, (Calmet) and those can never truly serve the invisible Deity, who despise their fathers, his visible images on the earth.

3:16.text: Sin. Or ill-nature. Greek, "instead of sins, (17.) a house shall be built up for thee."
Alms, prayer, and sacrifice for parents merit reward.

3:18.text: Of. Greek, "Like a blasphemer is," etc.
He who reviles his father, meditates blasphemy against the Deity.

3:19.text: Meekness. Here a new subject begins. Goodness begets love.

3:20.text: Greater. The dignity of a person should be the measure of his humility, (St. Ambrose, de virg. 31.) as the most elevated are the most exposed to pride. Humility is taught only by true wisdom and the gospel, Matthew 11:29. Philosophy may inspire us with the contempt of riches, etc.
Yet humility is the most indispensable duty, and no less essential than delivery to an orator.
All human greatness comes from God, who requires us to shew our gratitude by humility.

3:22.text: In, etc. The mysterious nature of God and providence, cannot be comprehended: and in many things we must confess our ignorance. Life is often spent in idle researches.

3:26.text: Suspicion. Greek Complutensian, "their vain suspicion hath deceived many and their wicked thought has ruined their judgment. Not having the apples of the eye, thou wilt be deprived of light; and being ignorant, do not speak." The latter sentence is marked by Grabe as wanting in his copy, as it is in the Vatican, if we may believe the London edition, (Haydock) which, however, is not an exact copy, no more than any of the other editions, of that famous manuscript.

3:27.text: Heart. Which fears neither God nor man.
Those who have not shewn mercy, can expect none.
The impenitent see their folly, like Antiochus, when it is too late.
They have loved the danger, which shall overtake them, Proverbs 1:26. He seems to refute those who believed in fate, and would take no precautions.
Those who live in sin, tempting God to the last hour, generally perish.

3:29.text: Wicked. Greek, "hard," obdurate in sin, like Pharao, Romans 2:5.
Sorrows. Or crimes, as the word often implies, and the punishment thereof.
"Sin, by its own weight, leads to another, (St. Gregory, Mor. 25:12.) and while custom is not resisted, a necessity arises."

3:30.text: Congregation. The proud or obstinate sinner will take no advice, Proverbs 18:3.

3:31.text: Understand. Greek, "shall devise a parable, and the ear of the hearer is the desire of a wise man."

3:33.text: Water, etc. He delights to instruct the attentive.
Resist. Greek, "shall expiate, or obtain pardon for sins," Daniel 4:24., and Luke 11:41. "Alms-deeds are a sort of baptism, which may be often repeated" to advantage.

3:33.src: St. Chrysostom, hom. liii., and lviii.
3:33.text: "When we bestow any thing, it is not of our own, but the gift of Christ. We give bread....He, the poor, renders us the kingdom of heaven." (St. Jerome in Psalm cxxxiii.) God becomes our debtor.

3:33.src: St. Augustine, City of God 21. ultra.[last chapter]
3:33.text: But then charity and repentance must accompany our alms.

4:1.text: Alms. Greek, "life," and of what is necessary to support it. Si non pavisti, occidisti. St. Thomas Aquinas (II. 2. q. 32:5.) quoting the offices of St. Ambrose, though the sentence is not to be found there.
Christians are not master of what is superfluous, no more than those who have the goods of the Church.
Turn. Greek, "draw not after thee indigent eyes."
Make not the poor wait long for relief, 2 Corinthians 9:7., Romans xii 8., and Proverbs 8:28.
Fames et mora bilem in nasum concitant.

4:5.text: For....anger, and behind, etc., is not in Greek. (Haydock) -- He thrice repeats the same injunction, Exodus 22:22.
Deal not cruelly with the poor; for God will revenge their injuries.

4:8.text: People in office must attend to all.

4:15.text: One. Or "sanctuary." They shall be honoured like priests, Isaias 61:6. Birth, rather than merit, gave a title to the Jewish priests; but those who are wise shall minister in a more glorious temple.

4:18.text: In temptation, etc. The meaning is, that before wisdom will choose any for her favourites, she will try them by leading them through contradictions, afflictions, and temptations, the usual noviceship of the children of God.
Greek, "for sideways or through crooked paths she shall walk with him at first, (19.) she," etc.
The narrow path leads to heaven, (Matthew 7:13.) and to virtue.

4:21.text: To him. And explain why she has treated him so roughly.

4:22.text: Enemy. Greek, "ruin." This road leads to eternal misery.

4:23.text: Time. For all things, Ecclesiastes 3:1. All depends on the proper season.

4:24.text: Ashamed, to ask for relief, (ver. 25.; Calmet) or to say the truth, as the martyrs have done. This is not in Greek.

4:25.text: Sin. Thus to refuse asking for what is necessary to support life, would be criminal.
Grace. When we refrain from doing things truly shameful, Ecclesiasticus 41.
To abhor sin is glorious; but to yield to it, through shamefacedness, or not to reprove it, is vicious.

4:27.text: Fall. Excuse not his faults, (Calmet) lest thou partake in them.

4:28.text: Hide, etc., is not in the Alexandrian copy, though inserted by Grabe.
Ostentation is blameable; still we must speak in defence of the truth.

4:29.text: Tongue. Greek, "by speech, wisdom shall be known, and instruction by the word of the tongue."

4:30.text: Lie. Greek, "of thy want of information." Yet Grabe and the Complutensian edition have lie, etc.
Always yield to the truth, (Calmet) and never tell a lie, but speak the truth at proper times.

4:32.text: River. Useless resistance is to be avoided; and when it is requisite to oppose the demands of the great, we must do it with reserve, ver. 33.

4:33.text: Strive. Greek, "unto death strive for the truth, and the Lord God will fight for thee."
The saints were modest, but intrepid.
All must rather lose their lives than act against justice or truth.

4:34.text: Works. Perform what thou hast promised.

5:1.text: Unjust. The translator styles riches unjust, because they often proceed from, or tend to injustice. They can afford no protection in death, Psalm 48:17., and Luke 12:19., and 16:9.

5:2.text: Heart. If no man can oppose thee, remember God's judgments.

5:3.text: How mighty am I? is not in Greek, though read by St. Cyprian and St. Augustine, Spec.
Sennacherib's vain boasting was soon punished, Isaias 37:36.

5:4.text: Rewarder. Greek, "is long-suffering, he will not let thee escape."

5:5.text: And add. Greek, "to add." Take not occasion from God's goodness to offend him.

Though you may have taken pains to repent, are you assured of being forgiven? (Ecclesiastes 9:1.) A relapse calls for greater severity, though the graces of God are without repentance, Romans 11:29. He does not recall his favours, but he hates the ungrateful, ver. 6.
Satisfaction must be made even after the sin has been remitted.

5:7.text: Looketh. Greek, "shall rest" in hell, to punish the abuse of mercy.

5:8.text: Day. A rabbin being asked when one must repent? answered, the day before death. But we know not when that will take place! Then, replied he, we must repent to-day.
"God has promised thee pardon, if thou repent, but he has not promised thee to-morrow."

5:12.text: The way. Greek, "thy knowledge or sentiment, and let thy speech be one," (Haydock) that men may depend upon thee.

5:13.text: Word. Complutensian adds, "of God." But the Roman edition has, "be quick to hear, and answer slowly," James 1:19. "It is safer to hear than to preach."
Constancy in virtue will obtain the promised peace.

5:14.text: Lest. Greek, "honour and infamy is in speech; and the tongue of man proves his ruin."
Nothing can be productive of better or of worse consequences, Proverbs 18:21.

5:16.text: Be. Greek, "ensnare not by thy tongue, (17.) for evil confusion is upon the thief, and great condemnation on the double-tongued. (18.) Be not ignorant in any thing, great or small."
The detractor is more dangerous than the thief. He pierces without being seen, Ecclesiastes 10:11., Proverbs 26:20., and Romans 1:29.

5:18.text: Great. Only examine the justice of the cause; or, according to the Greek, commit no fault of ignorance, nor say, "I did not think."
Acknowledge thy friend in his poverty, as well as in his greatness.

6:1.text: Instead. Greek, "and instead," etc. Syriac begins this chapter with the preceding verse, with which this is connected. Detraction will separate friends.

6:2.text: Extol. This conduct is inimical to true friendship, which requires that we should make allowance for one another's faults.
Like. Greek, "lest thy soul be torn away like a bull. Thou wilt eat," etc.

6:3.text: Wilderness. Thus was Nabuchodonosor humbled, Daniel 4:

6:5.text: Appeaseth. Greek, "and an eloquent tongue multiplies good words."
The affable gain our affections. Gideon pacified the incensed Ephraimites by a mild answer, Judges viii.

6:6.text: Counsellor. Only few are capable of this office, (Haydock) or of keeping a secret. Yet we must have peace, if possible, with all, Romans 12:18.

The Scythians condemned many friends, no less than many wives, (Luc. Tox.) and Aristotle (Eth. 9:10.) commends this maxim of Hesiod: mete poluxeinos met azeinos; "neither to have too many guests, or intimate friends, nor to be without any."

6:7.text: Get. Literally, "dost possess a friend, possess him in trial," (Haydock) as the Greek also has it. But the Hebrew term kanah, means likewise, "to acquire," and a friend ought to be chosen with judgment. It is too late to try him after he has been received.
One must try much before a companion be chosen, that he may be afterwards preserved.
"Possess not friends quickly, but those whom thou hast obtained, reject not with disgrace."

6:8.src: Cicero; St. Augustine, contra Acad. iii.
6:8.text: Trouble. Such are interested friends; but true friendship is a kind and perfect agreement in all divine and human affairs."

6:9.text: "The closest alliances, being broken, produce the most bitter enmities."

6:12.text: Humble. "Friends must have a respect for each other."

6:13.text: Friends. Such as have been just described. Of these the maxim of Bias may be true, that people should "love as if they were to hate," at some future period.
Entire confidence becomes those who are friends indeed.

6:14.text: Defence. Jonathas and Chusai saved David.

6:16.text: And immortality, is not in Greek.
But shews the meaning of life is this place; as a true friend will not cease to give good advice for eternity.
But even in this world, nothing can be more advantageous.
Amicus magis necessarius est quam ignis et aqua.
Him. Cicero himself says, "friendship can subsist only among the virtuous." Yet these, judging others by themselves, are more easily imposed upon, and ought, therefore, to address themselves to God.

6:17.text: Be. He will instill into his friend sentiments of piety, if he have them not before. Amicitia similes invenit aut facit. "Pythagoras desires that in friendship one should be formed of many."

6:18.text: Wisdom. A good education will, at last, bring forth fruit, though the passions may choke the good seed for a time.
"Take wisdom for the provision on thy journey, from youth to old age."

6:22.text: Trial. Such stones were used to try people's strength, (Zacharias 12:3.; Calmet) or to try gold.
The Syriac explains it of a precious stone. But the first idea is preferable. Many will not so much as attempt to become acquainted with wisdom and piety.

6:23.text: Name. Perhaps the author may compare the Greek word Sophia, (Calmet) with Tsopuie, (Haydock) "hidden," or with the Greek term, zophos, which means "darkness." See Ecclesiasticus 43:8,. and 46:1. The original Hebrew test is lost, so that we cannot determine to what word allusion is made. See Cornelius a Lapide, who has written the best commentary on this book.
But, etc., is not in Greek.

Many prefer learning before piety. But St. Augustine says, the unlearned rise and take the kingdom of heaven, while we with our learning, devoid of heart, (or charity; Haydock) behold we fall into the dirt.

6:28.text: Thee. She will even seek thee first, Wisdom 6:14., Proverbs viii., and Matthew 7:7.
Gotten. Continence does not here signify being chaste, (Calmet) though this is one of the fruits of wisdom, Wisdom 8:21., and Galatians 5:23.

6:30.text: Firm. Literally, "bases of virtue;" (Haydock) which is not in Greek, and rather embarrasses the sentence; (Calmet) though it may signify, that if we serve the Lord with fidelity, our building will never be overturned.

6:35.text: Wise. Literally, "prudent." The Latin has this epithet, because old people are not always such, though it may be expected of them.
"While wisdom increases, all other faculties decrease."

6:36.text: "The very meeting of the wise is of advantage; and thou mayst learn something of a great man, though he open not his mouth."

7:2.text: Evils. Greek, "injustice." Evil communications corrupt good manners, (Haydock) or the punishment of sin follows its commission.

7:3.text: Seven-fold: more abundantly, Osee 10:11., Job 4:5., and Galatians 6:7.
The wicked shall receive the fruit of their wickedness.

7:4.text: Honour. Ambition is the source of ruin to men and empires, James 3:1.

7:5.text: God. Greek, "the Lord, and affect not wisdom (sophizou) before the king."

Of ourselves we are despicable; and kings love not those whose superior talents seem to eclipse their own. David's valour excited the hatred of Saul, and Alexander "was indignant that Antipater (his general; Haydock) had conquered, thinking his own glory was diminished by that which another acquired."

7:6.text: Integrity. A judge is exposed to many dangers, 2 Paralipomenon 19:6.

7:6.src: St. Chrysostom, hom. xxxiv. in Hebrews xiii.
7:6.text: What will become of those who push themselves forward?

7:8.text: To sin. Correct not thy severity by too great indulgence, nor entangle thyself with the sins of othres, as with a chain, Isaias 5:18.

7:9.text: Mind. If thou hast committed a fault, despair not, but pray, (ver. 10.) avoiding presumption, ver. 14.

7:12.text: Soul. Whether the person have incurred a fault or not, we ought to shew pity, reflecting on our own frailty, 1 Corinthians 10:12.

7:13.text: Devise. Literally, "plough." But Hebrew Charasch, means also to machinate.
Brother. We cannot hence infer, with Grotius, that the Jews allowed themselves to deceive strangers.

7:14.text: Good, but very pernicious, (Matthew 5:37., and Apocalypse 21:8.; St. Augustine, Mend.) though some have thought that lies might be told to prevent evil.

7:15.text: Repeat not. Make not much babbling by repetition of words, but aim more at fervour of heart.
A supplication ought to be drawn up in a concise manner.
Judges and orators should speak with due reserve. Prayer must be unceasing: yet many words must not be used to inform God of our wants. Our Saviour seems to allude to this text, Matthew 6:7.

7:16.text: High. Genesis 2:15., and 3:18. The patriarchs and the most famous Roman generals followed this employment.

7:19.text: On the flesh, is not in Greek. Christ appears to have had this passage in view, Mark 9:44. Many suppose that both allude to the fire which burnt dead bodies, etc., in the vale of Hinnon. But all allow that the inextinguishable flames of hell are meant. Whether the fire and worms be corporeal or not has been questioned: the Greek Fathers, and the Church of Florence, generally maintain the negative, and the Latins assert it on better grounds. St. Thomas Aquinas adopts the opinion of many of the Fathers, who explain the worm to denote the remorse of conscience. Yet many take it to be a worm, though not like those with which we are acquainted. Horreo vermem mendacem et mortem vivacem. (St. Bernard, cons. v.) See St. Jerome; Isaias lxvi.; Cornelius a Lapide; St. Gregory, Dial. 4:29.; St. Augustine, City of God 20:10.; St. Ambrose vii. in Luke xiv.)
Fire and the worm of conscience are both eternal.
The punishment at least is such, and more intense than we can conceive.

7:21.text: Wife. The virtuous never approved of divorcing any but the incorrigible.

7:27.text: Man. The Jews in general embraced the married state in their youth. St. Paul prefers virginity, 1 Corinthians 7:8.

7:28.text: Hateful. To marry such a one would be an occasion of divorce, which is always odious.

7:29.text: Groanings, in child-bed, and (Calmet) during thy infancy.

7:33.text: Priests. Greek, "priest, and give him the portion which is enjoined thee, the first-fruit, and for negligence, and the gift of the shoulders, and the," etc., ver. 35.

Arms. That is, with all thy power: or else by arms (brachiis) are here signified the right shoulders of the victims, which by the law fell to the share of the priests. See ver. 35., (Challoner) Exodus 29:28., and Leviticus 7:22.

7:34.text: Few offerings, if thou be poor.
Be alone with the priest when thou dost confess, and imitate the small number.

7:36.text: Poor. They and Levites are always to be invited, Deuteronomy 12:19., and 14:26.

7:37.text: And restrain not grace from the dead. Withhold not from them the benefit of alms, prayers, and sacrifices. Such was the doctrine and practice of the Church of God, even in the time of the Old Testament. And the same has always been continued from the days of the apostles in the Church of the New Testament.
Meat was also placed on the tombs of the deceased, for the benefit of the poor, Tobias 4:18.

7:38.text: It was customary to join those who mourned for the dead, and to offer them meat, and endeavour to comfort them.

7:39.text: Love, being approved both by God and man.

7:40.text: End. Greek, "things;" death, judgment, and either hell or heaven.
This consideration is a powerful preservative against sin.
For who would dare to offend his judge, if he believed that the next moment he would be arraigned before his dreadful tribunal?

8:2.text: Bring. Greek, "overweigh thee," (Haydock) or turn the balance of the judge (Calmet) against thee, by money, (Haydock) which sometimes will corrupt kings. It is better, therefore, to avoid all law-suits.

8:4.text: Fire. Ver. 13., and Proverbs 26:20. Such contests are full of danger.

8:5.text: Family. Or "ancestors." They will be accused of having neglected thy education. People generally associate with those of the like dispositions.

8:13.text: Sinners. Greek, "a sinner, lest thou be burnt in the fire of his flame,["] when he shall be enraged.
This is not the time for reprehension.
The envoys of Ezechias prudently answered nothing to Rabsaces.

8:14.text: Words. Thus the Pharisees attacked our Saviour, Matthew 22:15. In popular assemblies it is dangerous, but sometimes requisite to oppose bold declaimers, Ecclesiasticus 9:25.

8:16.text: Power. Thou must consider the debt as thy own, Proverbs 6:1.

8:20.text: Love. Greek, "keep the word secret. (21) Do no hidden thing before a stranger."
The skilful alone must be consulted.

8:22.text: Evil. Greek, "false kindness." The rest is omitted.

Samson was undone by Dalilia. When Pompey was going on an embassy, he was taken by king Gentius, who wished to extort his secret from him; but he put his finger in the flame of a candle, that he might see that all his attempts would be fruitless.

9:1.text: Lest. "A husband should teach his wife to be chaste by his own example." (Lact. Relig. vi.) Ipse miser docui quo posset ludere pacto Custodes eheu! nunc premor arte mea.

9:4.text: The same person is often given to both music and dancing, (Menochius) and these arts are very dangerous.
The dances in the East were more licentious than ours.

9:5.text: Gaze. It is not always possible to avoid seeing them; but the greatest caution is requisite, Job 32:1., and Matthew 5:28.
Jealousy and all dangerous occasions must be avoided.

9:7.text: Streets. Greek, "retired parts," where bad women dwell.

9:8.text: Up. Greek, "of a fine appearance." Grabe substitutes, "who has found favour."
The Fathers condemn too much dressing as a mark of levity and incontinence.

9:12.text: Nor, etc., is also admitted in many copies, though found in the Complutensian and Aldus edition.

Grabe replaces it on the authority of Clement (Paed. 2:7.) as he does many other passages, which are erroneously left out in several editions. (Prolog. t. 4. C. 3.) It would therefore be injurious to the word of God, if we were always to reject the additions in the Vulgate, which are not in the Vatican Greek copy; and they must not all at least be considered as interpolations.

9:16.text: Envy not. If thou hast faith and wisdom, thou wilt rather dread his punishment, Psalm 36:1., and Proverbs 3:31.
He is like a summer flower, that is quickly cut down.

9:17.text: Wrong. Greek, "applauses of the unjust. Remember that they will not be justified even to hell."
The world itself will finally approve only of virtue.
The wicked will be condemned both by God and man.

9:20.text: It. Greek, "Know what thou art....walking on the pinnacles of the city."
The same sense is given in the Vulgate, as people walking among armed men in a rage, are exposed to imminent danger.

9:21.text: Beware. Greek, "aim at." Examine the person to whom thou intrusteth a secret.

9:24.text: Artificers. All strive to excel in their profession.

9:25.text: -----Dummodo risum Excutiat sibi, non cuiquam parcet amico.

10:1.text: The example of rulers is very powerful.

10:2.text: Therein. Regis ad exemplum totus componitur orbis.
As a whole city is infected with the vices of princes, so it may be corrected by their good behaviour,

10:3.text: Rulers. All who wish to live under just kings, Proverbs 29:4.

10:4.text: It, as he did David, Job 34:30., and Daniel 2:20.

10:5.text: The scribe. That is, the man that is wise and learned in the law.
It also denotes an officer, Judges 5:14. God must give light and success.

10:6.text: "Caesar used to forget nothing but injuries."
This at least is the character of a great man.

10:7.text: Men. It invades the rights of the former, and disturbs the repose of mankind.
All. Greek is obscure, "and from both proceeds injustice;" (Calmet) or, "it shall act unjustly."
Pride attacks both God and man.

10:8.text: The ambition and luxury of subjects (Cato) and the negligence of the rulers, bring on confusion.

10:9.text: Man. The desire of plunder, or of glory, occasions revolutions.
Covetousness is the root of all evils, and causes people to abandon the faith, 1 Timothy 6:10.

10:10.text: Bowels, and would have no compassion for others or for himself. Some Greek copies leave out as far as sale, but Grabe replaces the omission from the Complutensian.

10:14.text: Thus Lucifer and Adam fell by pride. (St. Augustine, City of God 12:6.) All sin, being a contempt of God, springs from pride, (Prosper. contemp. 3:3.) and from an inordinate self-love.

10:15.text: Sin, because man abandons God's law, and falls into all misery.
The proud easily yield to all sorts of iniquity.

10:16.text: Disgraced. Greek, "hath taken an exemplary vengeance, and hath," etc.
Them, as he did the giants, Sodom, Nabuchodonosor, etc.

10:17.text: "Thales being asked what was difficult to be seen; replied, a tyrant grown old."

10:18.text: Nations, the Chanaanites, who submitted to the yoke: or rather the Hebrews.

10:19.text: Foundation: overturning Sodom, Babylon, and even Jerusalem, for their sins.

10:28.text: Wise. When Diogenes was exposed to sale, and asked what he could do, he answered, "I know how to command free men."
Joseph and Daniel obtained authority by their wise conduct.

10:31.text: Desert. Let not avarice deprive thee of the necessities of life, nor do any thing beneath thy dignity, Luke 21:19.

10:33.text: Glorified. Greek, "honoured on account of his knowledge, and the rich is, " etc.

10:34.text: And. Greek omits this sentence: (Calmet) yet Grabe has, And the man who is without honour in wealth, how much more so will he be in poverty?

11:1.text: Men. Merit is preferable to high birth, as it surmounts difficulties.
Those who humble themselves, like Daniel, or fall under the oppression of others unjustly, as Joseph did, shall be exalted by God.

11:2.text: Look. "The most robust is farthest removed from wisdom."
The beauty of the soul must be regarded. (St. Ambrose, Virg. 3.) (Calmet) Forma bonum fragile est, quantumque accedit ad annos Fit minor, et spatio carpitur ipsa suo.

11:3.text: Sweetness. Honey was more esteemed before sugar became common. As the little bee produces such sweetness, so the less beautiful often by their abilities surpass the comely.

11:5.text: Throne. Greek, "pavement." Dionysius II, tyrant of Syracuse, was forced to turn schoolmaster at Corinth.
Crown. Abdalonymus, a gardener, was placed on the throne of Sidon; (Curt. 4.) and several Roman emperors had been of the meanest condition. (Pliny, [Natural History?] 35:18.) David was a shepherd.

11:6.text: Others, like Samson, Sedecias, Bajazet, etc.

11:7.text: Man, as David did Miphiboset, 2 Kings 16:4. Constantine the Great grieved for having put his son Crispus to death on too weak evidence; and Theodosius repented for the sentence he had rashly passed on the inhabitants of Thessalonica. Even though the sentence should happen to be just, it would be wrong to pronounce it too hastily.

11:8.text: Discourse. This is a mark of impertinence, Proverbs 18:13.
Let others finish their sentence, and wait if there be any elder or better informed to speak.

11:10.text: Escape. God will grant thee riches without so much solicitude (Lyranus) or rather meddle not with too many things, as that is the way to succeed in none, and destroys all repose.

11:16.text: Sinners. They are all born in sin. But some appear to have worse dispositions than others, Psalm 57:4.
God is not the author of iniquity.
He punishes in order to save, though he be offended by the wicked choice of man.
Blindness of heart and obduracy are punishments of sin.

11:17.text: Ever. All things work together for the good of the elect, Romans 8:8.

11:21.text: Covenant. All the Israelites partook in the covenants entered into between God and their fathers, which became their own by circumcision, as we become entitled to the alliance of Christ by baptism.

11:22.text: Place. If God bestow not riches, it may be for thy advantage.

11:24.text: Fruit. Prosperity was a sign of God's favour, under the old law. Now nothing is more equivocal, and the poor are declared blessed.

11:26.text: Myself. Neither the poor nor the rich can be independent of God.
Perhaps no one dares to hold this language. But many act as if they thought themselves gods, and some have claimed divine honours, 4 Kings 18:34., Isaias 14:13., and Ezechiel 29:3.

11:28.text: Death, in old age, (Grotius) or rather after the soul's departure. The pagans had not this consolation. Their hopes were confined to the earth; and as they saw many honest people miserable, they introduced a chimerical idea of happiness, consisting in virtue alone. The views of Christians are chiefly bent on future rewards.

11:29.text: Hour. The last of life. It demonstrates the vanity of past pleasures. Even a momentary toothache will banish the recollection of such delights.

11:30.text: Children. If they take wicked ways, it is a stain on their father's memory; and though the latter were blameless, it must greatly disturb their repose.

Children may here denote the works of man, which can only then be pronounced perfect, when he is incapable of falling from the state of virtue. (Haydock) ------Dicique beatus Ante obitum nemo supremaque funera debet.

Solon inculcated this truth to Croesus, and when the latter was about to be burnt to death by Cyrus, he called thrice upon Solon's name; the reason of which being told the conqueror, he took pity on the fallen king, and treated him with great respect.
These sages only regarded the goods of this life. But the Christian's real happiness commences after death. Mors....gratio missionis est.

11:36.text: Own. So Herod destroyed his wife's family, and Paris stole Helen.

12:1.text: To whom. All must not be admitted into the house, nor treated with marks of particular esteem. Yet daily alms must be given without too scrupulous an enquiry, Luke 6:30.
It would be wrong to encourage sinners, who would run to their ruin. But we must relieve the penitent.

12:2.text: Lord. Christ rewards what good is done to his poor brethren.

12:3.text: For. Greek, (4.) "Give to the pious, and receive not the sinner, (6.) Do," etc.

The gospel enjoins us to do good to all. St. Augustine (Dort. 3:16.) and St. Thomas Aquinas (II. 2. q. xxxii. a ix.) explain this in a spiritual sense, that we must not partake in the crimes of others. We may also make presents to the virtuous, and pass over people of a different character, particularly when they would abuse our gifts. The honest poor must be preserved.

12:10.text: Rusteth. So his malice always returns, (Calmet) and he shews what he is.

Christianity does not blame due reserve in treating with those whom we know not, or who have formerly injured us. Though we must love them from our heart, yet we need not intrust our secrets to them, nor even to every friend. Joab always retained a hatred for Abner, and Absalom for his brother and father, though they dissembled their resentment.
We must love, but not trust them, being wise as serpents, Matthew 10.

12:11.text: Of him. Greek adds, "and thou shalt be to him as one who has rubbed a mirror, (of brass from the rust. It returns perpetually. Calmet) and shalt know that the rust is not removed for ever.

12:13.text: Beasts. Vipers, etc. Why should they familiarize themselves with such? Some Italians still handle serpents without fear, pretending that they are the descendants of St. Paul.

But he was never married, (1 Corinthians vii.; Haydock) and they are rather of the race of the Marsi, who formerly claimed the same privilege, having made use of some drugs to prevent the poison. The ancients believed that they could charm serpents.

12:16.text: Blood. Jeremias 41:6. Samson yielded to the tears of women, Judges 14:16., and 16:6.

12:18.text: Eyes. Is taken from ver. 16. It is not in Greek.
His tears are like those of crocodiles, which cry when they are going to kill a man.

13:1.text: Pride. We are naturally prone to it, and the friendship and manners of the proud will increase it.
If, therefore, the rich be not virtuous, it is dangerous to keep their company, (Worthington) both for this and the next world.

13:3.text: Kettle. Made of iron. It will presently be broken. So the poor must suffer, if they marry, or have society with those who are much above their rank.

13:7.text: Thou? Distrust those who promise much.

13:8.text: He will feast thee, while he wants thy service, or he will come to dine with thee, and thus reduce thee to distress, Proverbs 23:1.

13:10.text: Humbled. By foolishly attempting to rival the sumptuous feasts of the great. Ver. 9., and 11., are not in the Greek.

13:11.text: Wisdom. Depend not on others' bounty.
When the cause of God is at stake, be firm, like St. Paul.
Pusillanimity in a superior might cause him to neglect his duty.

13:14.text: Concerning, etc., is not expressed in Greek.
If a person be so weak as to disclose his secret to a great man, he will never be trusted by him.

13:17.text: Awake. Be cautious even in sleep, if possible; or seem not to hear what passes at court.

13:19.text: Himself. Similarity of manners and of condition is favourable to friendship.
The good and bad cannot be friends, their manners are so different.

13:21.text: Lamb. Christ has taught people of different tempers to embrace the faith, which shows his admirable power, Isaias 11:6.

13:25.text: "It is inhuman to push those who are falling."

13:26.text: Helpers. Who prevent his ruin, (Calmet) and turn his words to good sense.
Proud. Greek, "what should not be disclosed," aporreta.
Their "fault increases, being fed by applause."

13:28.text: Clouds. People suppose that the rich must possess genius.
Exception of persons hinders many good counsels, and promotes evil.

13:30.text: Ungodly. The abuse of riches is alone blameable. All things are clean for the clean, while the wicked perverts every advantage.

13:32.text: Greek, "a cheerful countenance is the vestige of a heart in good things, (Haydock; prosperity, Menochius) and the discovery of parables is the effect of laborious enquiries."

14:3.text: Envious. Who will neither use his wealth himself, nor bestow it. The true use of riches is to take a sufficiency, and to relieve the poor.

14:4.text: Such misers are continually derided, but will never be cured, Ecclesiastes 2:18., and 6:2. An old proverb says, "The miser doth nothing right but when he dies."

14:6.text: Envieth. By refusing himself the necessaries of life, he rejects God's gifts, and scandalizes his neighbour.

14:7.text: Wickedness. Persisting covetous till death, as is commonly the case.

14:11.text: Offerings. Make a proper use of what God has bestowed.

14:12.text: The covenant of hell. The decree by which all are to go down to the regions of death, (Challoner) which hell frequently denotes.
Under the old law, all went down into some part of hell.
Hath been. Greek, "hath not," etc. We know not when we shall die, though all must taste of death, (Haydock) and therefore we must make haste to do good.
Shall, or "is, he shall die the death," (Genesis 2:17.) as the Greek has this sentence, ver. 18. (Haydock) Certo veniunt ordine Parcae, Nulli jusso cessare licet.

14:15.text: Sorrows. Greek, "toils," or what thou hast amassed with much care.

14:16.text: Take. Alms-deeds will ensure the remission of sins. This usury is lawful. We must also keep up a good society with our friends.
Some read "love," or divert thy soul by lawful amusements. The argument is addressed to Jews and Gentiles.

14:18.text: Fade. Greek, "grow old like a garment. For the covenant from the beginning is, he shall die the death. Like a leaf flourishing on a bushy tree."
Homer has the same thought, Iliad vi.

14:20.text: End. The fruits of the Spirit are alone immortal, ver. 21., and Galatians 6:8.

14:27.text: Glory. As under the miraculous pillar, Exodus 24:17. He who shall abide with wisdom shall have nothing to fear.

15:1.text: Good. Greek, "these things, and he who possess the knowledge of the law will find her," (Calmet) or, "he who keeps the law, will receive her."
All who resolve to be virtuous, will have God's grace, which preventeth them, and continueth to afford them assistance.

15:2.text: Married. Literally, "from virginity." (Haydock) Those who have been espoused in youth have the most durable love for one another, Proverbs 2:17., and Malachias 2:14.

15:5.text: She. Or Greek, "he shall open his mouth, (6.) he shall find joy and a crown of exultation, and shall inherit," etc.
Church, or assembly of the people. In both, the wise shall be heard with respect.

15:9.text: Praise. Or Greek, "a proverb," Proverbs 17:7., and 26:7.
David invites even dragons to praise God, but sinners never.
The person who preaches well, and lives not accordingly, injures God.

15:10.text: Wisdom. Greek, "it was not sent to him, (Calmet) or he was not commissioned by the Lord. For praise shall be spoken in wisdom, and the Lord will give him success."

15:11.src: St. Augustine, Grat. et. lib. ii.; Calmet
15:11.text: She. Ignorance and vice are defects, and consequently not the work of God, but of the perversion of free-will. See James 1:13., and Ezechiel 33:11.
Beza says, that God ordained Adam's fall, but for a good end; and decreed justly what men unjustly perform, (ref. Castel. ii.) which is refuted here, and Deuteronomy 7:11., etc.

15:14.text: Counsel. Many explain this of Adam, before his fall, when he stood not in need of asking continually for God's assistance.

But if we examine the context, it shews that man, in his present state, is declared inexcusable if he yield to sin, as he has still free-will, which may avoid it, with the grace of God, (Calmet) which is always ready to support us. Greek subjoins, (ver. 16.) "if thou wilt, thou shall keep the commandments, even to execute faithfully the good pleasure. (17.) He." etc.

15:15.text: Precepts. These would never be imposed, if man were not free.

15:17.text: And fire. Or, good and evil, as the Vulgate explains it, (ver. 18.) though the Greek be silent.
The law written on the heart, as well as on the tables of stone, shews man his duty;; and he has free-will to comply with it, Deuteronomy 30:15., and Jeremias 21:8.
He may enjoy every advantage of life, denoted by these two elements. (Lyr.[Lyranus?]) An quod in his vitae causa est, haec perdidit exul His nova fit conjux; haec duo magna putant.

15:21.src: St. Augustine, Grat. et lib. xx.
15:21.text: Commanded. He only permitted the wickedness of Semei, (2 Kings 16:10.) and the persecutors of his people, whose hearts are already bent on evil.

16:1.text: Rejoice. Greek, "desire not an useless multitude of children," etc.

16:2.text: Labours. All will end in ruin which springs from corruption.
Achab and Gedeon had seventy sons, but all were presently exterminated, while Abraham had a numerous posterity by his son Isaac.

16:7.text: Out. They shall perish by civil and foreign wars, (Calmet) and by God's indignation, Deuteronomy 32:23.
They who yield to the fire of concupiscence, will feel God's wrath for ever.

16:9.text: Of their word, is not in Greek, but may imply the effrontery of the Sodomites, who were not ashamed of openly professing their brutality, Ezechiel 16:49.

16:11.text: Six hundred thousand footmen, etc. Viz., the children of Israel, whom he sentenced to die in the wilderness, Numbers xiv., (Challoner) and Exodus 12:37.

16:14.text: Off. The virtuous shall be rewarded, Romans 2:6. Greek, "the patience of the impious shall not delay." He shall soon be punished, (Haydock) while he who gives only a cup of cold water to the poor, shall find a recompense, Matthew 10.

16:15.text: And, etc., is not in Greek, the different editions of which vary.

Instead of this sentence, Grabe inserts from the Complutensian, "the Lord hardened Pharao, that he might not know him, that his surprising works might be manifest under heaven. To every creature his mercy is evident, and he has divided his light from darkness with adamant.["]
His counsels are unsearchable, and the good are entirely separated from the reprobate, 1 Timothy 6:16., and Luke 16:26.

16:16.text: High. God would not be God if he were indifferent about virtue. None of his creatures can be beneath his notice. All the world is but like a drop of water to him, Isaias xl 15, 22. He cannot be distracted.

16:20.text: God's threats and promises touch not the sinner.

16:22.text: Testament. The law, or the judgment, which God shall pronounce.

16:26.text: And their. The heavenly bodies continue their courses, (Grotius; Jansenius) and all things still subsist, (Haydock) being formed and preserved by an intelligent being. He refutes the epicureans.

16:29.text: Be. Greek, "they shall not disobey his order for ever."
After the sun, etc., had been regulated on the fourth day, God set in order sublunary things, ver. 30.

16:31.text: Forth, the glory and power of God upon the earth.
Before. Greek, "hath covered its (the earth's) face, and into it," etc.
All living creatures shall return to dust, Psalm 103:29.
Rational and brute creatures praise God by answering the ends designed by him.

17:1.text: And made. This is transposed in Greek to the end of the third verse.

The creation of other things had been specified before. Man was designed to be immortal, and was made like to God in his soul, which is spiritual, intelligent, etc. But sin caused his days to be shortened.
By original sin, he lost the justice which he had at first received.

17:4.text: Dominion. This is greatly diminished since the fall.

17:6.text: Evil. Man has understanding, and may merit, which brutes cannot.

17:7.text: Hearts. God will bring them to judgment, and has given them a law.

17:9.text: Life. The observance of which will insure life both now and for ever, Romans 10:5. This was the peculiar privilege of Israel, (Calmet) though the natural law had the same effect, and God had left none without sufficient light.
The law was for a trial of obedience, Genesis 2:

17:10.text: Everlasting. The gospel has perfected or fulfilled the law, Matthew 5:17.

17:11.text: Saw. When he gave the law on Mount Sinai.

17:12.text: Neighbour. Of whom the second table, or the seven last precepts of the law, speak.

17:13.text: Eyes. He conducted them in the pillar, and always watched over them. Roman Greek adds, "their ways are bent on evils from youth, and he could not." Complutensian, "for every man is bent on evils from youth, and they could not from stony hearts make them fleshy, (Ezechiel 36:26.; Calmet) for in the division of the nations of all the earth (14.) over," etc.

17:15.text: God. Complutensian adds, "he nourishes him as his first-born with instruction, and dividing the light of love, he does not abandon him."

17:15.src: Origen; St. Augustine; Psalm cxxxviii.; Glossa.; Deuteronomy 32:8.
17:15.text: But the Roman edition agrees with us. God declared himself king of Israel, (Calmet) and its guardian, while he intrusted other nations to angels.

17:16.text: Ways. He suffers them not to follow their own inclinations, like the Gentiles; but chastises them if they have done wrong, Acts 14:15.

17:17.text: Covenants. Greek, "injustices were not concealed from him, and all," etc.
God. Complutensian adds, "but the Lord being good, and knowing his work, neither left nor abandoned them, still sparing them," Hebrews 13:5.
This is a gloss omitted in the Roman edition, Psalm 77:34.

17:18.text: Alms, and all good works are placed in God's treasury, and as a ring which a person wears on his finger with the utmost care, Jeremias 22:24., and Canticle of Canticles 8:6.
Eye. Greek Complutensian adds, (Calmet) "dividing to his sons and daughters penance, (19.) And."
The Roman and Aldus edition agree with the Vulgate.
Head. Giving a crown of glory.
And shall, etc., is not in Greek. It implies that the wicked will be punished like Core.

17:22.text: Offend less: minue offendicula. That is, remove sins, and the occasions of sins.

17:24.text: And. Greek, "return to the Highest, and be converted from injustice, (for he shall lead from darkness to the light of health) and greatly abhor abomination. Who shall praise the most high in hell, instead of the living, and of those who make a return of praise, or confession? Praise perisheth from the dead, as from one who has had no being. The living and sound of heart shall praise the Lord. (28.) How," etc.
We cannot merit perseverance, but must pray for it incessantly.

17:25.text: Go to the side, etc. Fly from the side of satan and sin, and join with the holy ones that follow God and godliness.
Christ is styled the father of the age to come, Isaias ix.

17:29.text: Immortal. We all stand in need of a pardon.
And. Greek, (30.) "what....and it fails. And flesh and blood shall study evil. (31.) He."
We cannot exact absolute perfection from any mortal. But still a person may be watchful, and trust in God's mercies, Ecclesiasticus 18.

18:1.src: St. Augustine, de Gen. ad Lit. 4:33.; Worthington; St. Thomas Aquinas, 1:p. 974. a. 2.
18:1.text: Together, as to their substance, though they received different forms successively, as Moses relates. Both writers are divinely inspired.
Some suppose that all things were really formed in an instant, and that the order described by Moses is only as we should conceive it.
This passage only means that God alone was the creator both of the sun and of man, and without him nothing was made, (John i., and Psalm 32:15.) as he gave existence to all, koine, "in common."

And he. Greek, "and there is no other besides him who steers the world with the palm of his hand. And all things obey his will; for he is king of all, in his might, separating what is holy among them from the profane. (2.) He has enabled no one to," etc.

18:5.text: Added. The works speak for themselves, though man be silent.

18:6.text: Begin. God is so great and incomprehensible, that when man has done all that he can to find out his greatness and boundless perfections, he is still to begin: for what he has found out, is but a mere nothing, in comparison of his infinity.
It is best to adore him in silence and humility.

18:7.text: Evil. What can man do for or against God? Job 22:3., and Psalm 15:2.

18:8.text: Years. Seneca fixes on the same number; (Brev. Vitae. 3.) Macrobius on seventy, for the life of man; which nearly agrees with the author of Psalm 89:10.
It seldom happens that people exceed 70, or 100 years. But what is this compared with eternity?
Greek, "a hundred years are many....so are a thousand years in the day of the age," or of aionos, eternity.

18:9.text: Them. Human misery calls forth God's pity.

18:12.text: Flesh. It is infinite and disinterested. Man assists his neighbour expecting a recompense, and remembering that he may be distressed.

18:14.text: Judgments. To execute his orders. Here ends the discourse begun [in] Ecclesiasticus 14:22.

18:15.text: The manner of giving, enhances the value of the gift, or even surpasses it. (Calmet) -----Super omnia vultus Accessere boni, nec iners pauperque voluntas.

18:20.text: Medicine. This is more requisite and easy to do in the maladies of the soul, which are brought on by our own fault. Principiis obsta.
Mercy. Greek, "propitiation in the hour of visitation," (Haydock) or punishment.

18:21.text: Sick. Roman Greek adds, "by abstinence, and in the time of sins, manifest a conversion."
If we take precautions to avoid illness, why should we neglect the concerns of our soul?

18:22.text: Always. These admirable maxims seem copied from the gospel, Luke 18:1.
The same spirit dictated all the Scriptures.

18:22.src: St. Augustine, ep. 121. q. ad Prob.; Worthington
18:22.text: Those pray always who neglect not this duty at proper times, and are always resolved to do so.

Greek, "be not hindered from performing thy vow in good time, and wait not to be justified, (Haydock) or to put it in execution till death. Deuteronomy 23:21. Before thou takest a vow," etc. Examine well if thou intend to perform it.

18:23.text: God. Can we expect that he will hear what we do not ourselves? To approach his majesty, without repentance and attention, is presumptuous. We must ask God to open our lips, and do what lies in us.

18:24.text: Face, saying to the reprobate, Depart, etc., Ecclesiasticus 7:40., and Matthew 25:41.

18:25.text: Riches. By their good use, lay up a treasure in heaven, and be always humble, Ecclesiasticus 11:27.

18:26.text: Reflect on Aman and Mardochai. (Calmet) Quem dies vidit veniens superbum, Hunc dies vidit fugiens jacentem.

18:27.text: Thing. Mindful of the reverses of fortune, Ecclesiasticus 11:30., and Proverbs 28:14.
Sloth, and repent. Greek adds, "the fool will not observe the season."

18:28.text: Her. It is a rare thing to discern and to give due praise to merit.

18:29.text: And judgments. Greek, "full of accuracy." Some add the title "restraint of the soul."

18:31.text: Enemies. This motive will make the most impression on those who are slaves to their passions.

18:32.text: Small. Literally, "nor in those which are small."
Composed of the meanest citizens. Quarrels and sin are there almost inevitable.
Greek, "rejoice not in high living; nor beg for its symbol," (Haydock) or feast, in which each person contributed his share, sumbole, as the next verse implies.

19:1.text: Little. Small expenses, often repeated, will ruin an estate, (Haydock) and the neglect of venial sins is of the most dangerous consequence.
The best way to avoid great faults, is to guard against small ones.

19:2.text: Off. From God (Calmet) and religion. See Genesis 19:33., and 2 Kings 11:1., and Osee 4:11.

19:3.text: The venereal disease seems to be described.

19:5.text: Hateth. Greek, "resisteth pleasure, shall crown his life. He who guardeth his tongue, shall live without contention: and he," etc.

19:6.text: He. Greek omits this explication of the preceding verse.

19:8.text: Not. Only to God and his minister. Friends do not require to be informed of such things. Greek, "disclose not the conduct of others."
Be. Greek, "be not." Do not boast of sins which thou hast not committed, (Calmet) as St. Augustine did before his conversion.
In ordinary conversation to reveal our faults would be dangerous, but not in confession, or in religious orders, for advancement in virtue.

19:9.text: Hate thee. Whether thou hast spoken of thy own or of others' faults, he will distrust thee.

19:10.text: Burst thee. Like poison, or as new wine (Calmet) does a leathern bottle.
This expression well implies the eagerness which some manifest to divulge a secret injurious to their neighbour.

19:13.text: For want of coming to a timely explanation, many friendships are broken, owing to groundless surmises.

19:15.text: Monere et moneri proprium est verae amicitiae.

19:17.text: Him. Gentle means must be first tried, Matthew 18:15., and Galatians 6:1.

19:18.text: The Complutensian has several additions which are not in the Roman edition.
Grabe inserts them. But it is not necessary to mark here every such variation.

19:22.text: Unjust. St. James 3:15. describes true and false wisdom, Romans 16:19.

19:23.text: Wickedly. Greek, "in black," (Haydock) wearing the robes of mourning.

19:24.text: The hypocrite abstains from exterior crimes, only through fear.
False pretence of piety, is hypocrisy; and it is wrong for a superior to reveal his secret faults to his subjects. Reason must direct.

19:26.text: Countenance. Yet this is not always the case, ver. 28. We may sometimes condemn a person unjustly, on such outward appearances.

19:28.text: Lying. Greek, "a reproof which is not seemly; and there is one," etc.

20:1.text: Prayer. A friend's excuses must be heard.
Greek, "and he who apologises shall be preserved from ruin." Grabe adds, "how good is it for one who has been rebuked," etc., ver. 4.
Thus the guilty merit pardon, and the innocent obtain a greater reward, and satisfy for former offences.

20:2.text: Maiden. Or "woman," (Haydock) who has been entrusted to his care.
Such people are not free from concupiscence, which must be restrained by the virtue of chastity.

20:8.text: Hated. As the pests of society. See Cicero, off. 1.

20:9.text: Loss. The prosperity of the wicked must not be envied, Psalm 38:1, 8.

20:11.text: Glory. Galba "would have been universally deemed worthy of the empire, if he had not reigned."
Adrian VI thought the pontificate the greatest misfortune to which he had been exposed; and this observation was engraven on his tomb.

20:12.text: Sevenfold. As the merchandise was good for nothing, which the miser had obtained for a little money, thinking to save thereby.

20:13.text: Graces. Or favours bestowed with ill-nature.

20:14.text: His intention is full of guile, who flattereth and detracteth for the sake of gain.

20:15.text: Much. As Dido did. "This is to make a display of favours to excite hatred."

20:16.text: Hateful. He seems to distrust you, and will not permit his goods to be of any service.

20:17.text: Such is the language of the fool.

20:19.text: That, etc. Riches and poverty are both wrong to misers and fools. Greek, "he had as well be without any thing."

20:23.text: Shall. Greek adds, "not." Yet if a person has had the will to offend, he must repent, though he could not put it in execution.

20:24.text: Himself. Being afraid to beg for necessaries, or given too much.

20:25.text: Nothing. People are always displeased, when a promise is broken.

20:26.text: Man. "It is the vice of slaves."

20:27.text: Lying. He exposes honour and virtue, without making restitution.
He throws all into confusion, and we can trust him with nothing.

20:29.text: A. Greek adds a title, "discourses of proverbs."

20:30.text: And....exalted, is not in Greek but explains the meaning of the former sentence.

20:31.text: Make. Greek, "like a bit in the mouth, remove corrections," Exodus 23:8.

20:32.src: St. Gregory, Part. 3:26.; Matthew xxv.; Calmet
20:32.text: Both? He who, through a false humility refuses to take charge of others, resembles a man who locks up his corn in time of scarcity.
Wisdom must be shewn for the benefit of others.

20:33.text: Folly. He may still appear to have some sense, and injures none.
It is convenient to conceal faults, provided they be amended.

21:1.text: Thee. The just may offend: but the wicked relapse continually.

21:2.text: Hold. Greek, "bite," (Calmet) in the same sense.
Serpents sting in secret: so all sins inveigle the soul.

21:3.text: Lion. Yet only those are hurt, who consent to sin. The devil may go round about, (1 Peter 5:8.) and tempt; (Calmet) he cannot bite, except a man willingly expose himself, and come near, ver. 2.

21:4.text: Only the sovereign physician can cure it.

21:5.text: Riches. As they make enemies arise. Greek subjoins, "so the house of the proud shall be made desolate."
Roboam lost ten tribes by an insolent answer, and the Tarquins were expelled for their pride.

21:6.text: Speedily. God is the protector of the poor, Psalm 10:5.

21:7.text: Sinner. He rejects all medicines. How shall he be cured?
They who fear God, will examine their actions when they are warned.

21:8.text: By him. And to avoid his reproaches. Greek, "knoweth when he is ruined."
He is more reserved, and escapes the dangers of talkativeness.

21:9.text: Winter. This season is unfit for building; so the man who borrows, may be forced to pay again (Calmet) before it is convenient for him.
Injustice will bring on ruin, Jeremias 23:13. Some Greek copies have, "stones for a heap over his tomb," as Achan and Absalom were treated. Reputation, or goods ill-acquired, will not continue long.

21:10.text: Fire. All their grandeur will end in smoke.

21:11.text: Stones. It is broad and easy, Matthew vii.

21:12.text: Thereof. Practice makes perfect. A painter may produce a better piece by this means, than one who is more acquainted with theory, (Calmet) and neglects it.

21:15.text: Bitterness. True wisdom promotes the general good.

21:16.text: Of life. Which never fails, John 4:14., and 7:38.

21:20.text: All good people dislike senseless talk, and approve of what is edifying.

21:21.text: Fool. All is in confusion, like a heap of ruins.

21:23.text: Low. A smiling countenance is commendable, but loud laughter is to be avoided.
It causes too great a change, (Plato, Rep. 3.) and is a mark of folly, Ecclesiastes 7:5.

21:25.text: House. And meddles with the concerns of others.
Of the mighty, is not in Greek. The wise will not easily receive invitations.

21:29.text: The wise speak with reflection, Proverbs 16:23.

21:30.text: While the ungodly, etc. He condemneth and curseth himself; inasmuch as by sin he takes part with the devil, and is, as it were, his member and subject.
In vain does he lay the blame of his sins on the devil, ver. 3.
He condemns himself by imitating the wicked, Luke xix.

21:31.text: By all. Greek, "in the neighbourhood." The rest is omitted.

22:1.text: Sluggard. Who neglects his own and others' welfare.
Disgrace. He is still despised here, and punished eternally, Matthew 25:30.

22:2.text: Pelted. Greek, "compared to a lump of dung."

22:3.text: Loss. "I wish," said Augustus, "I had never married, or that I had died without children."

22:4.text: Inheritance. Her wisdom and economy, Proverbs 31:29. Women had no inheritance among the Jews, when they had brothers.

22:5.text: Bold. In speaking and acting without restraint.

22:6.text: Mourning. St. Jerome (ep. ad Julian) quoting this sentence, calls it "divine Scripture," Proverb 25:20.
Of time. Or "stripes and instruction well-timed, are wisdom."
Only the wise know how to correct with advantage.

22:7.text: Fool. Who is incapable of understanding. Those who have sense may be corrected, though they may have taken evil courses.

22:9.text: This? The Athenian philosophers and Festus derided St. Paul, Acts 17:32.

22:10.text: For the fool. In the language of the Holy Ghost, he is styled a fool that turns away from God to follow vanity and sin. And what is said by the wise man against fools is meant of such fools as these.
Their wicked life is worse than death.

22:13.text: Days. There was a longer time assigned for great men, Genesis 50:3., Numbers 20:30., and Deuteronomy 34:8.
Life. This is understood of those who have lost all reason, or sense of religion. St. Monica wept for her son, hoping that he would repent, as a certain bishop had comforted her.

22:17.text: Lead? Gold alone is heavier. But the fool is compared with lead, and is most insupportable.

22:19.text: Wood. Such were used in the walls of Jerusalem, (4 Machabees ii.) and of the temple and palace, 3 Kings 6:36., and 7:12.

22:21.text: Greek omits this comparison, as well as the 23d verse.

22:23.text: Not fear. This seems contrary to what goes before. But fools are sometimes intrepid even to harshness.

22:24.text: Resentment. Or "sense," (Haydock) by means of pungent reprimands.

22:25.text: Away. Greek literally, "will knock them down," dejiciet.

Deliberate provocations (ver. 27.) are pardoned with more difficulty than a sudden impulse of wrath, ver. 26. On such occasions we may withdraw our confidence, but not our charity and patience, Matthew 5:11.
A true friend will not be lost for a temporal damage; but he must not be slighted.

22:29.text: Inheritance. Greek adds, "for poverty is not always contemptible, nor is the senseless rich worthy of admiration."

22:32.text: Of him. Yet I will not follow their example.
If my friend has behaved ill to me, he will be the greatest sufferer.

22:33.text: Not. Psalm 140:3. Who can sufficiently guard his tongue?

23:1.text: By them. Viz., the tongue and the lips, mentioned in the last verse of the foregoing chapter.
We cannot resist of ourselves, and must therefore pray to God.
We have need of a good master to correct our eagerness in speaking.

23:2.text: Ignorances, etc. That is, that the scourges and discipline of wisdom may restrain the ignorances, that is, the slips and offences which are usually committed by the tongue and the lips.
The tongue is a restless evil, James 3:8.

23:3.text: Over me. The noblest motives are not always proposed first.

23:5.text: Eyes. God never makes such presents, but he permits us to fall in punishment of former transgressions.
Coveting. Greek, "from thy servants all giant-like affection. Vain hopes and desires remove from me; and thou shalt possess him who wishes to serve thee without ceasing."

23:6.text: Foolish, (infrunitae) is used in this sense by Seneca.
Greek has not this epithet. The title, "instruction of the mouth," follows, (Haydock) to ver. 20.

23:10.text: Them. Alexandrian Greek has only (9.) "swearing, (10.) and have not a custom of naming the Most High." Other editions have "the holy one."
Rash and false oaths are forbidden, Jeremias 4., and Matthew 5.

23:11.text: Slaves were forced to confess the truth by torments; free-men took an oath; and priests gave their word.

23:13.text: Dissemble. Greek, "despise." He will be punished, at least, by God.

23:14.text: In vain. Without a design to execute, (Grotius) or through levity, or rather falsely, Exodus 23:1., and Deuteronomy 5:11.

23:15.text: Opposite. Or equal. Greek, "clothed with death." He denotes blasphemy, (Leviticus 24:14.) or the invitation to idolatry, (Deuteronomy 13:1.) or calumny, ver. 17., and Leviticus 19:12.
In oaths God is called to witness, as being unable to deceive. But blasphemy attributes to him or to others what does not belong to them.

23:16.text: Merciful. Or true believers, Assideans, 1 Machabees 2:42.

23:17.text: Of sin. A Hebrew idiom, to imply something criminal. Those who have laid aside shame, are capable of any wickedness.

23:18.text: Men. Treat thy parents with no less regard, (Calmet) even though thou shouldst be exalted in dignity. (Lyr.[Lyranus?]) ---Thus thou wilt be rewarded, (Calmet) of if thou transgress, wilt be brought to judgment.

23:19.text: Nativity. Wish not that thou hadst not been born of such parents.

23:20.text: Life. For who will admonish him?

23:21.text: Two. The first is a passionate man, (ver. 22.) the second a libertine, (ver. 23.) and the third an adulterer, ver. 25.

23:22.text: Hot. With any furious passion, (Vatable) or anger, (Calmet) avarice, (Worthington) or lust.

23:23.text: Fire. Shameful diseases will come upon him, Ecclesiasticus 19:3. His brutal passion will never be satiated.

23:24.text: Sweet. He is not delicate in his choice.
All obstacles increase his irregular desires, Proverbs 9:17.

23:27.text: And. Greek, "the eyes of men are his dread."
Such is the folly of the adulterer!

23:30.text: And he. Greek, "and where," etc. He shall be stoned, Leviticus 20:10.
If carnal adultery be thus punished, how much more will spiritual adultery, schism, and apostacy from the Catholic religion?

23:38.text: Shall. Greek, "that thou shouldst be taken under his protection."

24:1.text: Wisdom. Greek title, "the praise of wisdom." Here the book of Ecclesiastes is imitated.
See also Proverbs viii., and Wisdom 7:24., and 8:1. The pagans boasted of their philosophy. But it was not to be compared with the true religion, which the Israelites possessed.
Own self. Literally, "soul." She alone is capable of this office.
In God. Referring all the glory to him.
This sentence is not in Greek.
The second person of the Trinity proclaims his own praises.

24:2.text: Power. The temple, rather than in the schools of Athens.

24:3.text: And. Greek, (5.) "I came out of the mouth of the Highest, (6.) and as," etc.

He alludes to the darkness which covered the earth, Genesis 1:2., and Job 38:9. Many explain this of the uncreated wisdom, born in the womb of the blessed Virgin [Mary]; and the Church, in her office, applies it to her in a mystical sense. But it seems literally to refer to the wisdom and grace granted to the saints, which enlightens every man, and presides over the creation.

24:7.text: Cloud. Guiding the Israelites (Exodus 13:21.) or near the throne of God, Wisdom 9:4.

24:12.text: Rested. Greek, "gave rest to my tent."
So Virgil says, requierun. flumina cursus.
God offers grace, but forces none to accept of it.

24:13.text: And take, etc., is not in Greek.
This may be explained of the true religion, or (Calmet) of the word of the Father.
In the Church only is effectual grace, (Worthington) or none will receive it, who refuses to hear the Church.

24:14.text: Him. As his priestess, directing all the ministers of the temple.
Christ is begotten by the understanding, and the Holy Ghost proceeds from love. Creation is not here taken strictly.

24:16.text: His. Greek, "of his inheritance, (17.) I," etc.

24:17.text: Sion. Beyond the Jordan. Greek, "Hermon," Deuteronomy 4:48.

24:18.text: Cades-barne, Numbers 20:1. Greek, "Engaddi," (Calmet) or Roman and Alexandrian edition, "on the sea shore."

24:19.text: In the streets, is not in Greek.
These trees flourish most in such situations, and people delighted to walk under them in hot countries.

24:20.text: Myrrh. Which distills through the bark, without any incision.

24:21.text: Onyx. A shell-fish in the Indies, which feeds on the spica nardi, and hence becomes odoriferous.
And my, etc., is not in Greek. Wisdom is compared to what was most esteemed.

24:24.text: I, etc. Grabe restores this verse, and, instead of the next, has, "but I am given to all my children, being born myself for ever, to those who are specified by him. (26.) Come," etc.
The text seems to be corrupt.

24:25.text: Life. Christ declares himself to be, the way, the truth, and the life. [John 14:6.]

24:27.text: Spirit. Greek, "remembrance." It has not the following verse.

24:29.text: Thirst. Without being ever disgusted, John 4:13.
Experience of carnal delights is quite the reverse.
The more grace a person has, the more he desireth and receiveth from God.

24:30.text: Sin. Or lose their labour. They shall be directed in the paths of life.

24:31.text: They. Greek, "all these things are the book of the covenant of the most high God, the law which Moses gave unto us, an inheritance to the synagogues of Jacob." Grabe puts in a different character from the Complutensian, "Lose not strength in the Lord, but stick to him, that he may strengthen you. The Lord Almighty is the only God, and there is no other Saviour." Then follows, (ver. 35.) "who," etc.
Everlasting. Provided his life correspond with his doctrine. No occupation can be more glorious.

24:31.src: St. Augustine, Confessions 11:2.; Calmet; Daniel xii.; Menochius
24:31.text: Let thy Scriptures be my chaste delights: let me not be deceived in them, nor deceive others by them.

24:32.text: Truth. They are equally certain, and no more is required.
He that loves has fulfilled the law.

24:34.text: King. Viz., Christ, who by his gospel, like an overflowing river, hath enriched the earth with heavenly wisdom.
Ever. The Lord (Haydock) is a king, (Carriers) who, etc., ver. 35.
The sceptre of David remained till the captivity, and the royal family continued in esteem till Christ. Yet David was only a figure of Christ, the eternal king.

24:35.text: Phison. Or Phase of Colchis, which rises in Armenia, like the Tigris and Euphrates, all which overflow their banks at the beginning of summer, on account of the snow melting.

24:36.text: Harvest. The snow of Libanus makes the Jordan swell at Pentecost, Josue 3:15.

24:37.text: Light. Giving to all the light of reason, and to Israel the law, Psalm 147:20.
Gehon. Or Araxes, which descends from Armenia into the Caspian sea, though some erroneously take it to be the Nile, (Calmet) which overflows at the same time as the Euphrates.

24:38.text: Of her. Christ was the first that had perfect knowledge of heavenly wisdom.
This is the privilege of God. Greek, "the first hath not perfect," etc.

24:40.text: Rivers of saving waters, on all, but particularly (Haydock) on Judea, ver. 42. Greek, "and like a ditch (dioryx. Calmet) from a river, and as an aqueduct, I came into paradise," (Haydock) like the four rivers aforesaid, Genesis ii.

24:44.text: Off. He alludes to the conversion of the Gentiles.

24:45.text: I will. Many Fathers explain this of Christ's descent into hell. But as it occurs not in the Greek, if may be considered as the tradition of the first Christians, who gave this version, (Calmet) or they found it in their copies.

24:46.text: Prophecy. Of no less authority. The prophets had ceased for some time before Christ.
Them. Greek, "to ages of ages. See," etc.

24:47.text: 1:Wisdom, or the author of this book, Ecclesiasticus 33:18., and Wisdom 7:13.

25:1.text: Pleased. Wisdom still speaks. Solomon often makes similar divisions, to make the deeper impression, Proverbs 30:15, 24.

25:4.text: Fool. Greek, "adulterer, devoid of sense," Isaias 65:20.

25:5.text: Age? Wisdom, says Bias, "is more durable than other possessions."

25:9.text: Things. Greek, "nine suppositions I have blessed in my heart," (Haydock) or thought that they may confer felicity. B the tenth will certainly do it, Ecclesiasticus 14.

25:10.text: Enemies. Not out of revenge, but for the glory of God. See Matthew 5:43.

25:14.text: Things. Greek adds, "for light (15.) he that," etc.

25:16.text: It. This is not in the edition of Rome, or of Aldus: but it is in the rest. Fear commonly begins the work of salvation, and love perfects it, 1 Timothy 1:5.
All happiness in this life is grounded on the fear of God.

25:17.text: Evil. Greek reads not this; but the Syriac does, omitting the two next verses.
Heresy is described under the idea of a wicked woman, concealing her malice.

25:21.text: Enemies. Which they wish, or may behold, Ecclesiasticus 18:31.

25:22.text: Head. Or poison, as the Hebrew rosh signifies both.

25:23.text: Revenge is the passion of little souls, and of women.
Dragon. This is not exaggerated, if we reflect on the evils occasioned by Eve, Dalila, Athalia, etc.

25:24.text: Sackcloth. And is black when passion bursts forth, though women too frequently conceal their anger to take deeper vengeance.

25:25.text: Little. Greek, "bitterly." He cannot avoid hearing of his wife's misconduct.
She will raise him enemies on all sides.

25:26.text: Her. I could wish no greater evil to the worst of enemies, Ecclesiastes 7:27.

25:28.text: Beauty. Other qualifications of more importance must be regarded.
He should choose one who may be a companion and assistant in domestic concerns.

25:30.text: A. Greek, "If she bring a fortune to her husband," (Haydock) she will continually upbraid him with it, and cover him with confusion, ver. 29.
Lay-leadership in spiritual things is so unreasonable, that few heretics will endure it.

25:32.text: Happy. A lazy wife will give no content.

25:33.text: Die. Eve tempted her husband, and thus we are all involved in sin, 1 Timothy 2:14.

25:34.text: Water. Incontinency, Ecclesiasticus 26:15., and Proverbs 5:15. If water find a hole, it will presently flow away. Women were kept much at home in the East, as they are in many parts of Europe.
Retirement and silence are the characteristics of a wise woman.

25:35.text: She will. Greek, "cut her off from thy flesh." Grabe and Complutensian add, (Haydock) "give a bill of divorce or her dowry, and send her away."

26:1.text: Double. Uneasiness deprives a person of much comfort.

26:3.text: To a, etc., is not in Greek.

26:6.text: City. When all are charged with a crime, or when all rise up against a man.

26:9.text: With. Greek, "and the scourge of the tongue, which," etc.
Back-biting occasions the four evils aforesaid.

26:10.text: Woman. When oxen move, the yoke moves also: Thus heresy will suffer none to rest.
The yoke ought to fit the neck, and not be fastened to the horns.

26:11.text: Hid. She will be given to debauchery.
Romulus ordered wives to be slain, like adulteresses, who had drunk wine at home.

26:12.text: Eye-lids. Shewing her impudence, 2 Peter 2:14., and Isaias 3:16.

26:13.text: Love manifests itself by the eyes.

26:15.text: Hedge. Or "stake," palum, (Haydock) on which tents were fixed, Genesis 38:14.
Fail. Incontinence will at last ruin her health.

26:16.text: Bones. The Catholic faith is the ground of all virtues.

26:18.text: Worth. Literally, "exchange;" (Greek; Haydock) such a wife is above all price.

26:22.text: Holy. Made of gold, and placed in the sanctuary.

26:23.text: Soles. Greek, "heels." Complutensian, "solid pavement." A long addition follows in this edition, which is inserted by Grabe, (Haydock; but is not in the best copies. Calmet) instead of the following verse.

26:26.text: Poverty. The Romans provided for veteran soldiers, as Louis XIV did with great magnificence.
Greenwich hospital is for the same purpose.
Despised. Solomon makes the same complaint, Ecclesiastes 9:15.

26:27.text: To sin. He is less excusable, as he knows what he abandons, Jeremias 2:12.

26:28.text: Two. Greek, "scarcely is a merchant exempt from negligence, and the seller of wine, and eatables, (Grotius) or retailer (Voss. kapelos) shall not be justified from sin."

Negligence. That is, from the neglect of the service of God: because the eager pursuit of the mammon of this world, is apt to make men of that calling forget the great duties of loving God above all things, and their neighbours as themselves.
A huckster. Or a retailer of wine. Men of that profession are both greatly exposed to danger of sin themselves, and are too often accessary to the sins of others.
Lips. Innkeepers talk much to please, and thus fall into danger, Proverbs 10:19.
They are also exposed to lying, (Menochius) and to adulterate their merchandise.

27:1.text: Poverty. Greek, "the indifferent thing," as the Stoics represented money, Ecclesiasticus 7:20.
Sinned. Hence proceeds the danger to which little merchants are exposed. Involuntary povery is very miserable, Proverbs 30:9

27:2.text: Fast. Anacharsis styled "the market-place the receptacle of mutual cheating."
The seller praises his goods to get them off: (Horace) the buyer says it is bad, Proverbs 20:14. The next verse is a gloss omitted in Greek.

27:4.text: Fear. By this the soul is preserved in a good state.

27:5.text: Thoughts. The more he thinks on some things, the more is he perplexed.
After sin is purged away, there remains some defects like dust, Psalm 50:4.

27:6.text: A young man being brought to Socrates, that he might pass judgment on his dispositions, the philosopher ordered him to "speak."

27:7.text: As. Greek, "the fruit shews the dressing," etc.

27:9.text: Honour. The desire of justice is the sure method to obtain it. The rest is not in Greek.

27:11.text: Iniquities. They fall deeper, or sin entails punishment.

27:12.text: A. Greek, "the speech of the wise man is wisdom throughout: but," etc.
The fool always mixes something improper with what good he speaks.
Sun. The wise man preserves his virtue, whether it appear or not.

27:13.text: Go but seldom. Their discourse tends to promote iniquity, (ver. 14.) and blasphemous oaths, (ver. 15.) and bloodshed, ver. 16.

27:15.text: Ears. The Jews did so, when they heard blasphemy, Acts 7:56.

27:17.text: Mind. In Egypt, those who disclosed a secret to the enemy, were condemned to have their tongues cut out.

27:19.text: Him. He will never trust thee more, ver. 22.

27:20.text: This crime is like murder. The Romans sometimes solemnly renounced the friendship of those who had offended them, as Germanicus and Caius did that of Piso.

27:22.text: Because. Greek, "for one may bind up a wound, and an insult may be pardoned. But he who hath revealed secrets, hath lost all hope," (Haydock) or "confidence," ver. 24, Ecclesiasticus 22:27.

27:25.text: Off. Some Greek copies have, "he who knows him will depart from him." It is difficult to guard against a false friend, who winks as if he desired to please us, (Calmet) while he really seeks our ruin, ver. 26.

27:28.text: Traitors, in the dark, often wound their fellows, (Calmet) or themselves.

27:29.text: Setteth. Greek, "layeth a snare, shall perish in it."

27:30.text: Him. God will punish, when the sinner has perhaps forgotten his offence.
Such are often chastised here, and always hereafter.

27:33.text: Them. He shall feel the indignation of God, (Calmet) and shall repine.

28:1.text: Sins. To seek revenge out of rancour, or contrary to justice, is a grievous sin.
Retaliation was tolerated, (Exodus 21:24.) but love was not to be laid aside, Deuteronomy 33:35., Leviticus 21:17., Psalm 7:5., and Matthew 7:2.

28:2.text: Forgive. This charity enforces, when the offender is penitent, if the remission of punishment be not contrary to justice and discipline.
Matthew 6:12., and 18:32., and Luke 6:37. We all stand in need of pardon.

28:5.text: And doth. Greek, "who will expiate his sins?" Is God bound to receive his victims or prayers while he entertains such dispositions?

28:7.text: In his commandments. Supply the sentence out of the Greek thus: Remember corruption and death, and abide in the commandments, (Challoner) which condemn revenge, Exodus 23:4.

Complutensian, "and do not rage or menace thy neighbour with destruction and death; yea, stick to the commandments. Be mindful of the precepts, and be not angry with thy neighbour; and of the covenant," etc., ver. 9.
