28:9.text: Ignorance. He uses a softer term. In effect, most quarrels proceed from a misunderstanding. "Every sinner is ignorant."

28:12.text: Burneth. The more fuel, the greater will be the blaze.

28:13.text: And a, etc., is not in Greek. Proverbs 19:12., and 26:21.

28:16.text: Third. Who carries stories between friends, to set them at variance.

28:18.text: It, etc., is omitted in Greek.

28:19.text: Labours. Causing their husbands to suspect them.

28:22.text: Their own. Greek, "the." Detraction separates many friends.

28:26.text: Its. Greek, "it shall not take hold of the pious, and they shall not," etc.
Their virtue shall quickly shine forth.

28:28.text: Hear. Greek, "lo, hedge in thy possessions with thorns: bind up thy silver and gold."
To pay attention to detraction is as bad as to utter it.

28:29.text: Just. Greek, "a door and bolt," etc., as [in] ver. 28. If people guard their property with so much care, why do they make light of words?
These ought to be weighed with more attention than money, and we ought to spare no pains or expense on this head.

28:30.text: Tongue. Greek, "by them, lest thou fall before him who lays snares," Ecclesiasticus 29.

29:1.text: Hand. That is, he that is hearty and bountiful in lending to his neighbour in his necessity.
Foenus, in this book means simply "lending."

This is a work of mercy, and a sort of alms. Justice requires that the thing should be restored, and if any refuse on purpose, he is as bad as a thief. (Worthington) -- He who lends will receive usury from the Lord, Proverbs 19:17., and Psalm 36:21.

29:2.text: Time. The neglect of this causes many to be unwilling to lend.

29:5.text: Others kissed the face, neck, etc., Acts 20:37.

29:7.text: Found it. Seeming to make thee partaker of his good fortune, (Jansenius) or he will keep the other half as his own.

29:9.text: Injuries. Thus one loses both money and friends. Must we then never lend? If the person be poor, we must be ready to give.

29:11.text: And delay. Or literally, "and for an alms, drag him not along."
Let not the fraud of many deter thee from assisting the poor, for the sake of God and justice.

29:12.text: Commandment. Deuteronomy 15:7. Else where is your merit?

29:13.text: Friend. He is entitled to partake of all thy goods. If he be only thy fellow-creature, assist him; and God will reward thee, Matthew 6:19.

29:15.text: I never read that one who was liberal to the poor came to an evil death, as he has so many to intercede for him, whose prayers must be heard.
Works of mercy produce the best fruits.

29:18.text: Himself. Yet he will not engage his word for more than he can pay, (ver. 27., and Ecclesiasticus 8:16.) nor encourage the negligence of debtors, Proverbs 6:1.

29:19.text: Life. Or his subsistence, (chap. 19:28.) though some answered for the person of another, 3 Kings 20:39.

29:20.text: And. Greek, "will turn away the goods of his surety, (21.) and the ungrateful in mind will abandon his redeemer."
"All hate the ungrateful," (Cicero, off. ii.) and the Persians punished such with death.

29:23.text: Hence Solomon dissuades any rash engagements.

29:25.text: Things. Tax-gatherers may deserve to fall into this misery.

29:26.text: Not. Prudence requires that we should not ruin ourselves to help others.

29:27.text: Shame. Clothing is like a portable house.
Man stands in need of little, and those who are content will not need to borrow, ver. 29.

29:31.text: Shall. Or "has formerly entertained" those, who now reproach him, make him serve in the meanest offices, and at last cast him forth. It may also signify that people who receive ungrateful vagabonds, will be required to serve them, and had better keep them out, ver. 33.
Greek, "thou shalt entertain and give drink to the most disagreeable things."

29:32.text: Travellers carried their provisions with them.

29:33.text: Give. Greek, "depart, stranger, from the face of glory; for," etc.

29:34.text: Lender. Who demands his money, and upbraids his debtor.

30:1.text: And not, etc., is omitted in Greek. It may signify, and not beg, (Calmet) or steal.
The welfare of the country depends on the good education of children, which cannot be performed without correction, (Calmet) though this should never be used till more gentle means have been tried.
The neglect of correction proceeds from a misplaced tenderness, (Proverbs 13:24.) which in the end proves most prejudicial both to the child and to the public.
See Plato, rep. ii., and vii; Aristotle, pol. vi.; Cicero, off. 2:

30:2.text: He sees himself re-born in his son, ver. 4.

30:7.text: Wounds. To which he has exposed himself for his child's welfare, (2 Corinthians 12:15.; Raban.) or if he neglect correction, he will have to bewail the wounds which his son's imprudence shall occasion. Syriac, Vatable, Greek, Complutensian, "He who rubs, (Roman edition) cherishes his son," etc.
The eldest brother must take care of the rest, so as even to expose himself to danger.

30:8.text: Horse. A colt full of spirit, when properly broken in, will answer best, so a proper education corrects impetuous tempers.

30:11.text: Devices. Greek, "sins of ignorance." Youth is incapable of guiding itself, being destitute of experience, and too confident, Proverbs 29:45.

30:13.text: This is sometimes to be attributed to the neglect of education.

30:14.text: Evils. Health is better than riches.

30:15.text: Justice. This is the first of all advantages.
Thales pronounced him happy who was healthy and rich, and "whose soul was well tutored."
Greek, "health and a good constitution are preferable to all gold, and a," etc.

30:17.text: Rest. In the grave. He speaks not of the soul, Job 3:13.

30:18.text: Grave. The dead cannot partake of them, (Calmet) so neither can the sick of their great possessions.
It was customary to place meat on the tombs of the dead.
The pagans invited them to eat; (St. Epip. Ancor.) but the faithful intended it for the poor, who might pray (Calmet) for the deceased.
The kings of France were served at table till they were placed in the tomb, and in many monasteries the usual portion is assigned the dead for thirty days, and given to the poor.

30:21.text: Sighing. Thus meat is useless to those whom God visits with sickness. See Ecclesiasticus 20:3.

30:22.text: Sadness. For temporal things, but trusting in Providence, 1 Peter 5:7. Grief for sin is alone of service, 2 Corinthians 7:10.

30:23.text: A never. Greek, "the joy," etc., Proverbs 17:22., and 2 Corinthians 9:7.

30:24.text: Have. Greek, "love thy soul, and comfort thy heart, and drive," etc.

30:25.src: Hermas ii.; Mand. iii.; More Neb. 2:37.; Calmet
30:25.text: Many. Bringing on maladies, and driving people into despair, ver. 22. It was thought that the sad could not prophesy, 4 Kings 3:15.

30:26.text: Envy. Or jealousy. These passions banish joy. Envy is like rust.

30:27.text: Always. Greek, "will mind the meats which it shall eat."
It will feast and enjoy content. This verse and the following chapters are in a much less natural order in the Greek editions, till Ecclesiasticus 37.

31:1.text: The study of virtue and of the Scriptures removes temptations of the flesh.

31:2.text: Greek, "the solicitude of watching will drive away slumber, and a grievous malady will moderate sleep," (Grabe; Haydock) or "sleep will alleviate a severe illness," as experience evinces.

31:4.text: Poor. All have not equal success, Proverbs 10:22, and Psalm 126:1.

31:5.text: Justified. He is exposed to various temptations, 1 Timothy 6:9.

31:7.text: To it. Being a sort of idol, Colossians 3:5.

31:8.text: Nor, etc. Greek, (9.) who, etc. "Pride is the worm of riches."
"In delights, chastity is endangered; and humility in the midst of riches."
Not to transgress, on such occasions, is the greatest miracle.
Man has free will, and those are happy, who, by God's grace, do not abuse it.

31:11.text: In, etc. Alexandrian Greek, "and wisdom (Grabe substitutes the Church) shall proclaim his alms."

31:12.text: Be, etc. Greek, "do not open," (Haydock) shew not too much greediness.

31:14.text: Wicked. Jealous or avaricious, as if the person feared lest he should have to prepare the like sumptuous entertainment, Proverbs 23:1. Judas blamed the profusion of Magdalene, John 12:5.

31:15.text: Face. Or portion set before him, 1 Kings 1:5.

When. Greek, "wheresoever it, (Haydock) or he (Calmet) shall look, stretch not forth thy hand, and strive not with him in the dish. Judge," etc. Let the envious person (Haydock) or the master be served first.

31:19.text: Use. Greek, "in all act considerately. Eat like a man," etc., (Haydock) not like a beast.

31:20.text: First. Yet not too soon, lest the others should seem to be gluttons.

31:21.text: Of all. Greek, "of them. How," etc.

31:22.text: Wine. Greek specifies nothing, and to eat much is unwholesome.

31:23.text: Choler. Or the cholic. The intemperate, infrunito, (Calmet) Greek, "insatiable," cannot sleep.

31:25.text: Vomit. Some have omitted this term as indelicate; but the Scripture does not approve of intemperance, while it advises the preventing of a disorder. A man's stomach may sometimes be innocently overcharged. On such occasions, an emetic was prescribed. The ancients had not the same horror of it as moderns, Proverbs 23:8.
Cicero, addressing Caesar, says, Cum te vomere post caenam velli dixisses.

31:27.text: Quick. Intemperance and idleness are the great sources of illness.

31:28.text: Bread. And other provisions; while the miser is contemned, Proverbs 22:9.
Bread supports the body, and instruction the soul. If it be bad, it does the reverse.

31:30.text: Challenge. Not to fight, or rather (Haydock) to drink, Isaias 5:22.

31:31.text: Rebuke. Or disorder. Greek, "prove," (Haydock) or "lay open."

31:32.text: Taken. Greek, "is as good as life," etc.

31:33.text: With. Greek, "destitute of wine, (35.) and this was created to," etc.

31:35.text: And not. Greek, (36.) "sufficient wine taken in season is the," etc.

31:37.text: Anacharsis said that "the vine bore three clusters; the first of pleasure, the second of drunkenness, the third of discontent."

31:42.text: Reproach. Alexander [the Great] transfixed Clytus for so doing.
Again. By the pernicious custom of drinking healths, which still prevails in some countries, (Calmet) and occasions people to get drunk.
Such abuses ought to be discouraged by every one.

32:1.text: Ruler. Or king of the feast, alluding to an ancient custom, Esther 1:8.
Humility is most requisite for those in power, and it is most difficult to contemn vain honours, when enjoyed.

32:3.text: Contribution, towards the feast, (Menochius) or company. This sentence is not in Greek, "rejoice on their account, and for the excellent disposition, mayst receive a corner."

32:5.text: Music. Which usually accompanied the meals of the rich.
"A lyre is handed round, on which each one plays. When Themistocles confessed that he knew not how, he was deemed deficient." indoctior.

32:6.text: Words. Some Greek copies have, "where there is a concert, pour," etc.
And be. Greek, "and utter not wise sayings out of season."

32:7.text: Wine was not used at all meals.
After they were ended, the pagans sung in honour of Bacchus, or of great men.
But the Jews directed their hymns to God, Matthew 26:30.

32:9.text: Hear. Greek, (10.) "young man speak if there be wanted of thee; scarcely twice if thou be asked. Sum up thy speech, much in few words. Be knowing and still silent. (11.) In the," etc.

32:13.text: Thee. Greek implies to be equal.

32:14.text: It comes forth at the same time as the lightning, but travels slower.
Young people must leave the table, and take recreation.

32:17.text: Bless. This is taught by the light of reason, (chap. 5:7.) and the omission before or after meals betrays a want of faith and gratitude, particularly in Christians.
Replenisheth. Greek, "inebriateth thee with his goods."

32:20.text: Light. They shall advance in virtue and glory, Proverbs 4:18.

32:21.text: Excuse. Literally, "comparison." "He will bend the law to his desires." Thus heretics interpret Scripture.

32:24.text: Without counsel; or "by himself," (Calmet) cum eo.
And. Greek, "do nothing without counsel, and in acting do not repent," (Haydock) but execute what has been prudently devised.
God directs; yet he would have us to ask advice.

32:25.text: Rugged way. The end of which is unknown, Luke 14:28.
Lest. Greek, "and keep a guard over thy children. In every good work confide in thy soul; for," etc.

32:27.text: In faith. That is, follow sincerely thy soul in her faith and conscience, (Challoner) when it is well informed. Greek, "in every good work believe thy soul; for," etc.
Act not in opposition to it.
Rondet and Grotius would read, "in every work believe in God with all thy soul."
All that is not of faith is sin, (Romans 14:23.) inasmuch as it is against conscience.

33:1.text: Evils. God will make all turn to the advantage of the elect.

33:3.text: To him. God will fulfill all his promises.
Greek continues, "as the interrogation (Haydock) of the Urim, (delon. Calmet) prepare what to say, and so thou shalt be heard. Put on instruction, and thus reply."

33:5.text: The wicked turn from one vice to another, as heretics devise many errors, not having their heart established in grace, Hebrews 13:9.

33:6.text: Him. And will be unmanageable, (Menochius) when he has any thing to ridicule.

33:7.text: Another. God's will alone appoints one to be holy or fine; and another to be stormy, or dedicated to labour.
And one. Greek, "and all the light of the day throughout the year proceeds from the sun."

33:8.text: The sun. Greek, "and he distinguished the seasons and holidays (10.) some," etc.

33:10.text: Thus all men are by nature equal. Yet what difference do we not find in their condition? Some live in obscurity, who might have shone upon the throne; and this is God's will.

33:12.text: Station. Exterminating the Chanaanites. God disposes of all with sovereign power and justice, Romans 9:29.

33:14.src: St. Augustine, Persev. xiv. n. 35, and Corrept. xiii. n. 42.
33:14.text: "The predestination of the saints is nothing but the foreknowledge and preparation of God's benefits, by which those are most certainly liberated who obtain their freedom. But where are the rest left; except in the mass of perdition, by the just judgment of the Deity?

33:15.text: Another. Lights and shades both contribute to form the beauty of a picture.
Antitheses adorn a discourse, as opposite things do the universe.
God will make the wicked subservient to his glory.

33:16.text: Of all. Solomon, Ezechias, etc., made various collections of similar maxims.
The books of the Machabees were alone written after this in the Old Testament.
Greek places what follows after Ecclesiasticus 30:26. These four verses may be regarded as a sort of preface.

33:21.text: Change thee. That is, so as to have this power over thee.

33:23.text: The pre-eminence. That is, be master in thy own house, and part not with thy authority.
Let not thy wife or servants rule in thy name.

33:25.text: They were bought like horses. Aristotle (oecon. 1:5.) gives the like instructions on their treatment.

33:27.text: A stiff. Greek, "the neck, (28.) torture," etc.

33:31.text: Faithful, is not expressed in Greek but must be understood.
Blood. Taking him prisoner at the hazard of thy life. The like misfortune might easily have befallen thee.
Seneca (ep. 47.) says, "live so with thy inferior, as thou wouldst have thy superior live with thee."

33:33.text: Thou. Greek, "on what road wilt thou seek for him?"

34:1.text: No dependence can be had on the pretended interpretation of dreams, which do not come from God; (Calmet) as some do, Matthew i., and Daniel 2:

34:3.text: Man. When he turns away from the mirror, it appears no more, James 1:23.

34:4.text: Unclean. This text has been abused, to prove that the sinner cannot confer grace in the sacraments.
But Christ does this by his ministry.

34:5.text: Deceitful. Greek, "divinations, and auguries, and dreams, are vain."

34:6.text: Travail. Such things cannot be explained. Twenty people will all give different interpretations.
High, as those of Jacob, Joseph, etc., were. Yet it is difficult to make the discrimination.
Homer admits of two sorts of dreams.

34:7.text: Them. The devil takes advantage of their weakness, and they have recourse to magic, etc.

34:8.text: Law, which forbids attention to dreams, Leviticus xix 26., and Isaias 8:20.
No part of the law shall perish; and some will always explain it truly.

34:9.text: What. Greek, "A man who has travelled, knows much," etc.
Thus Ulysses became so wise, (Homer) as well as the ancient philosophers, Pythagoras, etc.
The knowledge derived from books will not suffice.

34:10.text: Experienced. Greek, "much abroad, shall abound with subtlety. (11.) I," etc.

34:12.text: And. Greek, "And I know more than I announce."

34:13.text: For. Greek, "On account of these things I have been saved:" (Haydock) by my great experience, which gives weight to my instructions.

34:14.text: Is. Greek, "shall live. (15.) For....them. (16.) He," etc.

34:20.text: From falling, or when a person is fallen.

34:21.text: Mockeries. Some Greek copies have "gifts." Such unjust presents or sacrifices, God will abhor, Isaias 41:8., and Deuteronomy 24:15.

34:26.text: Bread. Greek, "a livelihood, killeth his neighbour; and he sheddeth blood, who keepeth back the hire of the workman."

34:28.text: Labour? So, if your sacrifices be ill-gotten, they will not be received; and if you relapse, you will be as bad as ever.

34:30.text: Dead. Literally, "is baptized by or from the dead;" baptizatur a mortuo.

St. Cyprian thought this text was peremptory against the baptism of heretics; and the Donatists were of the same opinion. They seem not to have read, if he touch him again, which entirely alters the meaning. St. Augustine admonishes them of this; though he explains it of the pagan rather than of the Jewish purifications, to which it refers, Numbers 19:11. See St. Augustine, contra Cres. 1:24., and 2:25., and contra Petil. 1:9., and St. Cyprian, ep. ad Quint.

34:31.text: Prayer. A relapse makes the former repentance useless, Matthew 18:33.

35:1.text: Offerings. He cannot otherwise observe the law.
Virtue is the most acceptable sacrifice.
The sacrifices of penance, of justice, and of praise, are to be preferred before all external ones.

35:2.text: The peace-offering and that for sin, as well as the oblation of fine flour and of praise, (which is the most perfect of all) are noticed.
External sacrifice must not be neglected.

35:4.text: This was the true spirit of the law.

35:6.text: Lord. The poor, who present a virtuous heart, are more acceptable than the rich, who are buried in sin, though the latter may offer the most splendid sacrifices.

God requires that all should do according to their abilities. If they have nothing, they must fulfill the commandments, and he will be satisfied. Some have inferred from Exodus 23:16., that the poor were rejected. But the meaning of the law is here explained.

35:10.text: Heart. Literally, "mind." Greek, "eye," (Haydock) free from avarice, 2 Corinthians 9:7.

35:11.text: Sanctify: "set apart" for the use of the ministers, and of the temple.
God will reward thee much more.

35:14.text: Wicked; defective, Leviticus 22:21., and Malachias 1:7. Greek, "curtailed, or ill-acquired," (Calmet) or "do not bribe" God; (dorokopei. Grotius) as the word is used [in] 2 Machabees. God will not regard our gifts, to let our offences escape punishment, Isaias 42:1.

35:19.text: For, etc., is not in Greek. God will protect the defenceless.

35:21.text: Prayer is personified, and presents itself before God.

35:22.text: Them, the Gentiles, etc., ver. 23. Greek, "will not delay to punish them, till he crush the loins of the unmerciful. And," etc.

35:23.text: Unjust Egyptians, Syrians, and Greeks, who then oppressed the Jews.

35:24.text: Our version seems to be taken from the Hebrew.

35:26.text: The. Greek, "mercy is beautiful," etc., Psalm 9:10.

36:1.text: Have. He has pointed out the conditions for prayer, and now he gives a model. The Jews were at this time dispersed and subservient to the Egyptians or Syrians, which greatly afflicted them.

36:2.text: That. Roman Greek omits the rest. But Grabe retains, "all nations which seek not after thee."
This prayer implies a prediction of the Gentiles' conversion, as the psalms do frequently in like terms, Psalm lxiv.

36:4.text: Sanctified, by chastising us, or by protecting our fathers in the desert.

36:10.text: End of our sufferings. The malice of our enemies is at the height.

36:11.text: Escapeth the sword, Deuteronomy 32:36. He foretells the event, or wishes that they may be chastised, in order that they may enter into themselves, and adore the true God.

36:12.text: Other lord. The Syrian and Egyptian monarchs sometimes claimed divine honours, Daniel 7:25., and 11:36.

36:13.text: That. Greek transposes the rest, and has only, and thou, etc.

36:14.text: First-born. Exodus 4:22. Wives, children, and servants, were called after their lord or father, Isaias 4:1., and 63:19., and Jeremias 7:10.

36:15.text: Rest, where the temple is built, 2 Paralipomenon 6:41., and Psalm 131:8.

36:16.text: Words, predictions, as heretofore; or with thy favours.

36:18.text: Thee. The Jews were more faithful after the captivity. The author seems to beg for the coming of the Messias.

36:19.text: People, which thou hast prescribed, Numbers 6:24.
Art. Greek, "Lord art the God of ages."

36:20.text: Another. Thus act discreetly in the pursuit of knowledge. The good may be distinguished from bad, as easily as venison can from other meats.
As there is a difference of meats, so must discretion be used in words, in the choice of a wife, etc.

36:22.text: Resist. Greek, "requite it," as it deserves.

36:23.text: Man. Yet in marriage the temper of the person is to be considered.

36:24.text: More. Love is the most violent of all the passions, and nothing can afford greater content than a virtuous and consoling wife, Ecclesiasticus 26:21.

36:26.text: Good, is not in Greek, but the context shews that it is necessary.
By concord small possessions increase, as by discord the greatest are lost.

36:27.text: Want. If he be not so at first, servants will soon ruin his affairs.

36:28.text: Rest, or abode with his wife and family. The Jews in general married, and such as delayed were deemed suspicious characters, Proverbs 27:8.

37:1.text: Death. Greek explains this: (Calmet) I mean (2.) "a companion and friend turned?" etc.
True friendship is most necessary, and false most dangerous.

37:5.text: Will. Grotius suspects that not is wanting. Yet, if such a false friend take up arms, it will only be (Calmet) for his own belly.
Not is ill-omitted in the Complutensian edition in the last part of the following verse.

37:7.text: Snare. Roman and Alexandrian Greek has at the end (Calmet) of ver. 11., "with him that suspecteth thee."

37:12.text: Treat not. The negation is omitted in the Vulgate, (Haydock) which speaks ironically in the three following verses. Greek, "consult not with him who suspecteth thee, and hide thy counsel from those who are jealous of thee. With a woman," etc. Consult not, is always understood. Among the Jews, polygamy occasioned much jealousy.

37:13.text: Nor. Greek, "with the merciless concerning kindness, with the sluggard concerning any work. With one hired for the year, concerning coming to an end:" sunteleias.
He will never finish his work, or think he has sufficient wages.

37:15.text: God, and is enlightened, as well as a good friend, ver. 16.

37:17.text: It. Greek, "more faithful than it," the well regulated heart.
It will give the best counsel, if we be not guided by passion.

37:18.text: Holy is not in Greek, but must be understood.
The soul or heart, which is attentive to God, (ver. 17.) will be the best guide.
All good advice proceeds from the Lord.
We must always have recourse to him, though we must use our best endeavours.

37:20.text: In. Greek, "Reason is chief of every work, and counsel must go before every action."
We must distrust our own lights.

37:21.text: Them. The heart gives birth to good or evil, which the tongue utters, Matthew 12:34., and 15:18.
Soul. What will science, without charity, profit him, if he should even gain the world?

37:23.text: Hateful. His sophisms are soon discovered. Greek, "there is one wise, hateful in his discourses: he shall be devoid of all wisdom."

37:25.text: Soul. Being replenished with wisdom, he communicates it to others, and obtains an eternal reward.

37:28.text: But. Greek, "And." The wisest must soon die: Even the race of Israel shall end: But the virtuous shall be for ever remembered, ver. 29.
The blessed in heaven live for ever.

37:30.text: Life. Attempt nothing above thy strength: or Greek, try what agrees with thy health or soul, (Calmet) and see thou give it not what is hurtful to it."
St. Paul (1 Corinthians xi.) exhorts every one to prove or try himself and his own conscience.

37:31.text: Soul. "We must not dispute about tastes."
They are as various as men's faces. All conditions do not suit every person.

37:33.text: Choler, or indigestion. "Gluttony has slain more than the sword."

38:1.text: The health of body and soul must be regarded.

38:2.text: King. Physicians were formerly kept at the king's expense.

38:4.text: Them, as he will all superstitious remedies.
"A crowd of physicians killed Caesar," said Adrian, on his death-bed. They often try experiments, and kill with impunity,
Yet we must not condemn the prudent use of medicine.

38:5.src: Tirinus; Menochius; St. Augustine, q. lvii. in Ex.
38:5.text: Many suppose that he alludes to the miracle of Moses, (Exodus 15:25.) who thus was supernaturally informed (Calmet) of the effects of a certain wood.

38:6.text: The. Greek, "that his or its virtue might be known by man."

38:7.text: And of. Greek, "Of these the maker of ointments shall compose a mixture and his works are not yet finished, when peace (health) from him appears on the face of the earth."
Diseases flee before him; so powerful are his medicines, that the very smell effects a cure.

38:9.text: Thee. We must neither trust too much in physicians, (2 Paralipomenon 16:12.) nor despise them, as this would be tempting God.

38:10.text: Sin. All diseases are in consequence of original sin, and many are inflicted for actual transgressions, John 9:2., and 1 Corinthians 11:30., and Numbers 12:10.

38:11.text: Then. Greek, "as being no longer. Yet give," etc.
Be converted and offer sacrifice, as if there were no hopes of life. Still do not despair.

38:13.text: Thou. Greek, "a sweet smell (success) shall be in their hands."

38:15.text: Physician. This state is very troublesome, and a punishment of sin, ver. 10.

38:16.text: Burial. Thus we must shew our respect for the deceased. The Jews also prayed for them, 2 Machabees 12:42.
"The care....and pomp of funerals are rather the consolations of survivors than of service to the dead."

38:17.text: For. Greek, "and shed burning tears of mourning, (18.) and lament for," etc.

Sorrow would be unsuitable for the saints, and useless for the damned, who being rebellious to God do not deserve our lamentations. Yet nature dictates, and people expect we should allow something to the first emotions of sorrow, (Calmet) for a day or two; and we may receive the consolatory visits of our friends, during the seven days of mourning, Ecclesiasticus 22:13., and John 11:19.
To avoid the evil reports of men, we must shew ourselves to be really sorry; yet we must not suffer grief to hurt our health.

38:19.text: And the, etc., is not in Greek. Excessive sorrow brings on sickness, and renders us unfit for any thing.

38:20.text: Substance; or what the poor has to live on, will seem sweet or bitter according as he is affected with joy or grief.

38:21.text: End. This is the chief utility derived from attending funerals.

38:23.text: Thee. A man on his death-bed, or already dead, is introduced speaking.

38:24.text: Him. Greek, "thyself." He is at rest, we hope; (Haydock) be thou so too.

38:25.text: A scribe; that is, a doctor of the law, or a learned man.
Sorrow (Haydock) and too much employment, are injurious to learning.
When pastors have leisure, they would do well to write, in imitation of St. Paul, etc.

38:28.text: Work: engraving on gold, or silver, or on precious stones.

38:29.text: Sitting. Such was the ancient custom of smiths, (Calmet) who carried all their implements with them, and laboured with much pain.

38:34.text: Glazing. The earthen vessel would otherwise spoil all but oil and water.

38:37.text: Dwell. Greek, "travel," to get a livelihood, (Grotius) or to acquire wisdom, (chap. 34:9., and 39:5.) and walk about like the ancient philosophers. They shall not be chosen magistrates.

38:38.text: Spoken. They have not leisure to attend the lectures of sages; (Haydock) nor are they ever quoted by them.

38:39.text: World, giving children to the commonwealth, (Menochius) and employing themselves in useful arts. The Jews would not allow magicians, players, etc., to reside in their country.
Craft. They pray that they may succeed in their employment, while some also strive to comply with their religious duties.
Applying. Greek refers this to the following chapter, "As for him who applies his soul and meditates on the law of the Most High, he will," etc.

39:1.text: Ancients. The Essenes (Josephus, Jewish Wars 1:7.) and Therapeuts (Philo, Comtemp.) were most famous for doing so. In general the Jews despise the learning of foreign nations: but some have applied themselves to it, particularly Philo, Josephus, etc., 1 Machabees 1:11., and 2 Machabees 6:11.)
Prophets. These required the utmost study, before the coming of Christ, 1 Peter 1:11., and Daniel 9:9.
The virtuous must follow both an active and contemplative life.

39:2.text: Sayings, or history of Abraham, etc.
Parables. This was most in vogue, 3 Kings 10.
But mechanics abstained from this study, Ecclesiasticus 38:38.
To meditate well, 1. knowledge; 2. virtue; 3. humility; and 4. hope, are requisite.

39:4.text: Governor of the province or army. The most enlightened were chosen, like Joseph, Daniel 1:4., etc.

39:5.text: Men. Travelling was very requisite, (chap. 34:11.) and history records the actions of the bad as well as of the good, for the instruction of the public.

39:6.text: Pray. Diligence and prayer are the means to acquire wisdom, Ecclesiasticus 4:13.

39:9.text: Lord, being in a sort of rapture, and enlightened by Him.

39:13.text: Generation. With what care do we not preserve the Scriptures? etc.

39:16.text: With. Greek, "as the moon in the first quarter," dichomenia. The Vulgate has read Diomenia, "divine transport." The author declares that he was inspired.

39:17.text: By. Greek, "Hear me, ye holy children."
He speaks to the children of Israel, the people of God: whom he exhorts to bud forth and flourish with virtue.

39:20.text: Voice. Greek, "songs of your lips, (Haydock) and with ancient lyres," etc.

39:22.text: Waters. At the world's creation, or at the passage of the Israelites. [Exodus 14:21., and Josue 3:13.]

39:23.text: Salvation. None can prevent the salvation of God's elect.

39:25.text: The greatest miracles cost him nothing.

39:26.text: Time. The veil shall be withdrawn, and Providence will appear. Greek, "all things are made for their proper use."

39:28.text: That. Greek, "as he changed the waters into saltness," (Haydock) at Sodom, (Calmet) "his ways are plain for the saints: so to sinners they are stumbling-blocks," ver. 30. The Red Sea gave a passage to Israel, and overwhelmed the Egyptians.
Him. God does all with grandeur. He poured his graces upon Israel, and overwhelmed the giants in the deluge.

39:30.text: Good and, is omitted in Greek. Before the fall, all was happiness. Now, the wicked have still some mixture of good, ver. 32.

39:31.text: Water and fire. These are requisite to prepare bread. The Romans refused them to the enemies of the state.
God gives the necessaries of life, which the virtuous use well, for their reward; and the wicked ill, to their ruin.

39:32.text: Evil. The good or bad use of them decides all, Wisdom 14:11., Romans 8:18., and Titus 1:15.

39:33.text: Spirits. Storms (Psalm 10:7., and 148:8.) good angels, (Genesis 19:11., and Isaias 37:36.) or rather devils: who, though created in holiness, fell, and became executioners of the wicked.
They abused their free-will, and are eternally punished.

39:34.text: Destruction, and final ruin of the impious, or at the last day.
Appease. God is pleased with the execution of justice.
Yet the devils cannot obtain a reconciliation; neither do they act to please God, having their wills obstinately bent against his, though they be forced to obey.

39:36.text: Beasts. Wolves, etc., with which the country was infested, Deuteronomy 7:22., and 22:24.

39:37.text: Feast. Greek, "rejoice," as at a feast, Ezechiel 39:16.
Word. They will be ready at the first sign.
All creatures, but the rebel angels and man, obey God.

39:38.text: Resolved. Literally, "confirmed" in this opinion by a divine light, ver. 16, 21.

39:39.text: Time. If we receive not at first, we must have patience.

39:40.text: That. Even sin contributes to manifest the justice and mercy of God; and we are not to judge of his works, but to praise him; as every thing is created for wise purposes, and evil proceeds from our abuse of things.

40:1.text: All misery (Calmet) is in consequence of original sin.
The life of man is a trial, Job 7:1., and 14:1.
Of all. The earth, (Calmet) which covers us, when all other things cast us away.

40:4.text: Purple. Literally, "hyacinth," (Haydock) or violet colour. Purple was more like scarlet.

40:5.text: Knowledge. He is disquieted by restless (Calmet) and frightful dreams, ver. 6.

40:7.text: Fear, or no reason for it.

40:8.text: Beast. Their instinct causes them to fear death, to fight, etc.

40:9.text: Moreover. Greek, "death," etc., are all destined for the wicked.

40:12.text: They shall not appear in the book of life.

40:14.text: Rejoice. The corrupt judge once rejoiced: but now he shall mourn.
The possessions and grandeur of the unjust shall end in a moment, like a clap of thunder, Psalm 36:36., and Proverbs 10:25.

40:15.text: And make. Greek, "they are unclean," (Haydock) and will produce no fruit.

40:16.text: Weed. Literally, "verdure." Greek Achei, (Haydock) properly denotes the rush, papyrus, which grows on the banks of the Nile, (Genesis 41:2.; Calmet) and was used for fuel.
Thus the wicked will presently (Calmet) be treated.

40:17.text: Grace. The beneficent will produce fruits, like those of paradise, Genesis 2:8.

40:18.text: That is. Greek, "and of one that."
In it. Greek, "and more than both is one discovering a treasure," (Haydock) who finds himself rich, without labour or injustice.

40:19.text: Both. Thus Judith, Zenobia, etc., ennobled their husbands, Proverbs 31:10.

40:20.text: Wisdom. Virtue gives more content to the soul than corporal pleasures do to the body. It brings us near to God.

40:22.text: Green. "The sight of no colour is more agreeable to the eyes."
The Church is more excellent than the synagogue.

40:23.text: Husband. Their union is closer, and they can best assist each other.

40:25.text: Counsel. By this the Romans conquered the world, 1 Machabees 8:31., and Proverbs 11:14.

40:26.text: Heart, and make people presumptuous; while piety gives real courage, Psalm 26:1.

40:28.text: The virtuous man is covered with glory.

40:29.text: Indigent. Greek, "live not by begging," being too lazy to work, or incurring this punishment for thy sins, Leviticus 26:16., Psalm cviii., and Deuteronomy 15:4. Involuntary poverty is a most severe scourge.
All are bound to labour for what is needful; (Worthington) some by prayer, others by trade, etc.

40:30.text: Meat. Such is the condition of the parasite, (Calmet) and of the beggar.

40:32.text: Unwise. Some read, "impudent man," with the Greek.
Fire. He shall feel the inconveniences of poverty; (Haydock) yet will not work, being lost to all shame.
Plato (Leg. 11.) orders magistrates to "clear the country of such an animal," zoou, (Haydock) and the Lacedemonians suffered no beggars among them.

41:1.text: Death is terrible to all, but most to those who live comfortably.
O vita misero longa, felici brevis.

41:3.text: Sentence, pronounced on Adam and all his posterity.

41:5.text: Flesh. "It is a great consolation to share the fate of all."

41:6.text: What. Greek, "why wouldst thou refuse to submit to the?" etc.

41:7.text: Life. Thou wilt not be asked how long, but how well thou hast lived. No one will then envy thy long life.
It will be in vain to plead that the length or shortness of life has occasioned thy sins; for God does all with justice and for the best, if men would use rightly his benefits.
Thou wilt not repine at having lived too short a time.
An evil life will be alone condemned, Wisdom 5.

41:8.text: Ungodly. They adopt the wicked manners of their parents (Calmet) and companions, and thus become still more criminal. (Haydock) oetas parentum pejor avis tulit Nos nequiores, mox daturos Progeniem vitiosiorem.
This was terribly verified in the three French assemblies, which overturned the Church and state. (The year of our Lord 1793.) See Barruel.

41:10.text: Father. He is rather their executioner, (Calmet) and would have been less cruel, if he had murdered them while they were innocent, Wisdom 12:10.

41:12.text: Portion. It would have been better for them never to have existed, Matthew 26:24. This [is] the case of heresiarchs.

41:13.text: It is their destination (chap. 40:11.; Calmet) and choice.

41:14.text: Body. The death of the body is bewailed in the just, but that of the soul also of wicked people calls for our tears. They will soon perish, Psalm 9:7.

41:15.text: Great. The concern which all have for a good name, is one of the strongest proofs of the souls' immortality, Proverbs 22:11.

41:17.text: Peace, with docility, (Matthew 11:25.) or in the midst of prosperity, be on your guard, Ecclesiasticus 20:32.

41:19.text: Have a shame, etc. That is to say, be ashamed of doing any of these things, which I am now going to mention: for though sometimes shamefacedness is not to be indulged, yet it is often good and necessary; as in the following cases.
Disciples ought to esteem what their masters teach, though they do not perceive the reasonableness of what they assert.

41:20.text: Opinion. Grotius corrects the Greek, "It is not laudable to be incredulous in all."

41:21.text: Mother. Thy misconduct seems to redound to their dishonour, as if they had not given thee a proper education.

41:22.text: People. They will stone thee. Remember the fate of Roboam, 3 Kings xii.

41:24.text: And of. Literally, "out of respect for the," etc. De veritate Dei et testamento.

The same words occur [in] Ecclesiasticus 42:2.; whence Jansenius thinks they have been transferred hither. Most refer them to what goes before. Blush for lying, which is contrary to the truth of God; and for the other sins which injure his covenant.
Meat. This posture betrays idleness or pride.

41:25.text: Harlot. The eyes must be chaste, Jeremias 9:21., and Matthew 5:28.
Kinsman, when he is poor, and refusing to assist him.

41:26.text: Turn. Greek, "of taking away a portion and gift," made already to God or men; (27.) "of considering attentively another's wife; of being too busy about his," etc.

42:1.text: Men. Here the Greek concludes the former chapter very properly, (Haydock) as we must be ashamed of doing these things, and not of those which follow.
"Of repeating [and] of the speech which thou hast heard, and of revealing secret words; (things.; Haydock) and thou shalt be truly bashful, (aischunteros.; Calmet) and find favour with every man."
Thereby. In such cases we must shew a holy impudence, like Jeremias 1:10, 18.

42:2.text: Covenant. We must be ashamed of transgressing these, (chap. 41:24.) but not of complying with them. This is the first duty of man.
Ungodly. Blush not to oppose such a judgment, (Calmet) though all the judges should be against thee. Follow no collusion of man to do evil.
Pass sentence without respect of persons, (Deuteronomy 1:16.; Calmet) whether the parties be thy companions, or only travellers, ver. 3.

42:3.text: Friends. Be not ashamed to leave something to thy friends, though relations may grumble; or execute thy friend's will exactly.

42:4.text: Little. Be scrupulously exact in thy dealings, but not afraid of advancing thy fortune by lawful means.

42:5.text: Buying. This regards magistrates, who must correct such frauds. Greek, "of the money (Calmet) to purchase from merchants."
We may examine whether the coin be good, and strive to get things as cheap as we can.
Bleed. Great severity was formerly used, Ecclesiasticus 30:12.

42:7.text: In. Not out of distrust so much, as to keep servants honest. All who have written on economy give the same rules.

42:8.text: Men. So Daniel discovered the malice of the two ancients, Daniel 13:46. Admonish the aged not to enter into disputes, or strive with the young.

42:9.text: An unmarried woman was styled Halma, or "hidden," Isaias 7:11.
Some read adultera, (Sixtus V; Jansenius) instead of adulta, improperly. It was deemed shameful for a parent not to have his daughter married soon, 1 Corinthians 7:36.

42:10.text: Barren. And thus fall into contempt, Deuteronomy 7:14. If she committed adultery she must die; or if she were only suspected, she must drink the waters of jealousy, Numbers 5:17. Husbands would often seek a divorce, on such occasions; which would involve the father in fresh difficulties.

42:11.text: Multitude, who will accuse thee of negligence.

42:12.text: Body's. Literally, "man's." But females are included in this term.
It was also necessary to guard the Greeks, and pagans in general, against a criminal affection for boys.

42:13.text: Man. Greek, "woman." Malice is natural to her. Beauty becomes her ruin, as well as that of others.
The Fathers compare women to a loadstone.
Men who come near them, are greatly exposed.

42:14.text: Better, etc. That is, there is, commonly speaking, less danger to be apprehended to the soul from the churlishness, or injuries we receive from men, than from the flattering favours and familiarity of women.
Josephus (contra Apion ii.) attributes this sentence to Moses, and prefers the worst man to the best woman, as Euripides does in Clement.
Reproach. This explains what sort of good the woman aforesaid has done; she had fostered the passions of men, and brought them to shame, which all the malevolence of an enemy could not have done.
The company of women is therefore more dangerous, Ecclesiasticus 9.

42:15.text: Remember, or publish. He praises God and the great men of his nation, to the end of the book, (Calmet) or to Ecclesiasticus 50:29.
I have only abridged what the sacred Scriptures relate.

42:16.text: Work. The world displays God's glory and power, Psalm 18:6.

42:17.text: Saints, in heaven, or rather the Israelites, Psalm 147:20.

42:19.text: Signs. Literally, "sign," the order of the stars, etc.
Yet God does not study these things, to discover what will happen. He is the author of nature.
From his works we are to form some idea of his greatness, Job xxxviii., etc.

42:25.text: Another. By this opposition the world subsists, according to Linus, Epimenides, etc. Hanc Deus et melior litem natura diremit. ([Ovid,] Met. i.) See Ecclesiasticus 33:16.

42:26.text: He. Greek, "one thing establishes the good of another," (Haydock) serving as a counterpoise.
Black colours cause the white to shine forth. Youth is guided by the experience of old age.

43:1.text: Shew. We cannot behold these things without admiration of God.

43:2.text: High. The sun is the most excellent of all irrational creatures, affording light (Worthington) and heat for the production of things; (Haydock) whence Aristotle (de Anima ii.) styles it "father of men and of gods." Yet it is inferior to man, being devoid of reason. All creatures manifest God's majesty; the little as well as the great.

43:5.text: Hastened. Some Greek copies read "slackened," alluding to Josue 10:13.

43:6.text: In all. Complutensian Greek, "to stand guard." Other copies agree with the Vulgate, though embarrassed.
World. To mark out the seasons, Genesis 1:14., and Psalm 103:19.

43:7.text: Day. The lunar system prevailed only after the captivity. The Passover was celebrated on the 14th of the moon of Nisan.

43:8.text: Name. Meni is used for the moon, (Jeremias 65:11.) which resembles the Greek men, "month." This is also derived from mene, "the moon." We know not what Hebrew words were used, Ecclesiasticus 6:23. The two former may both come from manah, "to divide."

43:9.text: Armies. The stars, etc., are compared to a camp or army.

43:11.text: Judgment. The angels are not pure before God; (Job 4:18., and 15:15.) or rather the stars obey his orders, Baruch 3:34., Judges 5:20., and Psalm 118:91.

43:12.text: Brightness. Mille trahit varios adverso sole colores.

43:14.text: Judgment, to punish the wicked, Exodus 14:24., and Isaias 37:36.

43:15.text: Birds, sent to give information by letters, as the Egyptians still do.

43:16.text: Broken, as from huge mountains, Job 38:39.

43:18.text: Strike. Greek, "upbraid, (Drusius) or make the earth fall in labour," odinesen.

43:19.text: Locusts. "They cover the cornfields with a destructive cloud."

43:20.text: Shower, as if God were about to drown the world. The melting of snow occasions dreadful inundations.
Its whiteness sometimes deprives people of their sight.

43:21.text: Thistles. Greek, "stakes," pointed as it were with steel.

43:23.text: Burn. Cold destroys verdure no less than fire, Genesis 31:40.

43:24.text: Overpower it. Greek, "give joy; (25.) with," etc.
Warmth and rain restore beauty to the earth, after the horrors of winter.

43:25.text: Lord. Roman Greek, "Jesus planted it;" which is a mistake for nesous, islands.
"He planted islands in it." Grabe thus corrects the Alexandrian copy, though it agree with the Roman and Aldine editions.

43:26.text: Thereof. Psalm 106:23. "He who has not sailed has seen nothing evil."

43:28.text: Is. Greek, "his angel gives a prosperous journey."
If God be angry, all will go to the bottom.

43:29.text: All. Literally, "in all." Every thing obeys God, Ecclesiasticus 42:15., and Ecclesiastes 12:13.

43:30.text: Him. So the Greek reads. Vulgate seems to say, "boasting in all, what shall we be able to do?" What is man, fighting against the Lord!

43:32.text: His. Greek, "exalting the Lord, exert your power; and labour, for you will not come up to him: (35.) who," etc.
He is above all praise, Psalm 144:3.

43:35.text: From. Greek, "many hidden things are," etc.
The angels, and many secrets of nature, have not been mentioned.

44:1.text: Greek prefixes, "A Hymn of the Fathers." The author here imitates the third work of Solomon.
He had given rules for all virtues; and he now sets before us those who had put them in practice.
It was the custom to sound forth the praises of these fathers in the temple and synagogues, ver. 15.

44:2.text: Glory. Greek adds, "in them." He was the author of all their virtue, (Haydock) and has acquired much glory by their means, (Calmet) among men; though his essential glory can never increase.

44:3.text: In the. Greek, "futurity by their predictions." Kings, judges, and prophets are praised.
The virtuous are justly styled kings, Genesis xxiii.

44:4.text: The present. Greek, "the people by counsels, and by the knowledge of the office of scribes of the people. Wise in speech by their instruction. (5.) Seeking out," etc.
Prudence and eloquence ought to appear in a ruler.

44:5.text: Tunes. The Hebrews, and David in particular, were famous for music.

44:6.text: In. Greek, "established in power, living," etc., (Haydock) like Abraham, Ezechias, etc.

44:7.text: Generations. Literally, "races of their nation." But this is omitted in Greek.

44:8.text: Born. Greek, "some of them have left a name," (Haydock) or posterity, Deuteronomy 25:6.

44:9.text: Them. This may be understood of the wicked Israelites, or of the sons of Abraham by Agar, etc.

44:10.text: Mercy. Assideans, (1 Paralipomenon 6:41., and 1 Machabees 7:13.) or true Israelites.

44:14.text: Peace. The Jews were very solicitous about this, Genesis 35:8.

44:16.text: Into. Greek, "a model of penance to past or future generations."

The Latin fathers suppose that Henoch was translated to heaven, or to the earthly paradise. It is the tradition both of Jews and of Christians that he is still alive, and will come to oppose Antichrist, (Apocalypse 11:3., Genesis 5:22., and Hebrews 11:5.; Calmet) when he will preach penance, (Worthington) chiefly to the Gentiles, while Elias will address himself to the Jews.

44:17.text: A. Greek, "an exchange," to save mankind, Genesis 6:8., and 8:21.
Noe[Noah] was perfect, (Worthington) which does not exclude some human failings.

44:20.text: Multitude. His name implies as much.
He was father of all who believed in Christ.

44:21.text: Faithful. He received the sign of circumcision, and was ready to sacrifice Israel.

44:22.text: In his. Greek, "that the nations should be blessed in his posterity."

44:23.text: Earth. In Arabia, and from the Red Sea to the Euphrates. This was verified in David, Psalm lxxi., and Genesis 13:14.

44:25.text: Jacob. The promises made to Abraham were confirmed to Isaac and Jacob.
These were also blessed in Abraham.

44:26.text: Tribes. Giving him so many children, who inherited Chanaan.

44:27.text: Men. The Israelites, ver. 10. Greek, "the man," Joseph; though the Greek of the Roman edition would refer it to Moses.
Grabe corrects his copy agreeably to the Vulgate.

45:1.text: Men. Pharao, Jethro, and the whole nation of the Hebrews, whom he governed as a father, being the mildest of men, Numbers 12:23.[3.?] His life was a continued miracle, and he was honoured more than any prophet, (ibid. vi.[Numbers 12:7.?], and Acts vii.; Calmet) seeing God's works more clearly, but not his substance, Exodus xxxiii.

45:2.text: Saints, patriarchs or angels, as he saw God face to face.
Enemies. The Egyptians, Amalecites, and all who rebelled against God.
Cease. Removing the scourges which he had inflicted. His serpent devoured those of the magicians, who were confounded before him.

45:3.text: Glory, when he had passed by, Exodus 33:22.

45:4.text: Meekness, giving him these necessary qualifications to rule a rebellious people, ver. 1.

45:5.text: Heard. Greek, "made him hear his voice."

45:6.text: Life, by observing which, the Hebrews might live. Thus the trees of life and of knowledge were a sort of remedy against death and ignorance, Genesis 2:9.

45:7.text: His. Greek, "holy, like to himself his brother of the," etc.
He was the interpreter of Moses, and honoured with the high priesthood. Aaron and his posterity were bound to be faithful to the law, ver. 19.
Their priesthood was to last till Christ appeared, a priest forever of the order of Melchisedech.

45:9.text: Robe. Literally, "stole," (Haydock) which was a long robe, used in the East by both sexes.
Crowned. Greek, "confirmed" him in his dignity.

45:10.text: To the. Greek, "enclosing his legs, (Haydock; breeches, Calmet) and the robe hanging down to the feet, (podere) and the garment over the shoulders," (Haydock, ephod) which met at the breast, where the rational was fixed. See Exodus xxviii.
Many. Greek, "spheres, (Haydock; or pomegranates, Exodus 28:23.; Calmet) with many little bells," (Haydock) to admonish the people of his coming.

45:12.text: Man. Beseleel, who made the greatest part.
Greek, "of an embroiderer, with the rational of judgment, and the signs (manifestation. Calmet) of truth.
Thus the Septuagint commonly describe the Urim and Thummim, Exodus 28:6.

45:13.text: Their names were engraven on 12 stones.

45:14.text: Holiness, or "holy to the Lord," Exodus xxviii.
This regards all his attire. The high priest only used it in the temple, on grand festivals.

45:17.text: Some of the ordinary priests offered the daily holocausts.

45:19.text: Testament. It prefigured that of Christ, which lasts for ever, Hebrews vii.
Execute. Greek, "serve him, both by executing the priestly office, and by blessing the people," etc.

This was one of the high priest's functions, Numbers 6:23. The rest might offer sacrifice and incense, except on the day of expiation, (Leviticus xvi.) and on solemn festivals, though the Scripture is silent on the latter head.

45:21.text: Law. The most important causes were to be tried before the high priest, who, with his brethren, were the interpreters of the law, Matthew 2:6.

45:26.text: Eat. The skin of holocausts was alone given to the priests.

45:27.text: People. Having only 2,000 cubits round their cities, Numbers xxxv. The priests were thus interested to keep the people to their duty, as their own subsistence depended on the devotion and gifts of the latter.
They had no distinct territory, like the rest of the tribes.

45:28.text: Imitating. Greek, "by shewing a zeal in the fear of the Lord," Numbers 25:11.

45:31.text: That he. Or so he made a covenant with Aaron, that he, etc.

Greek, "and (Grabe puts according to) the covenant made to David....that the king's son successively should alone inherit, the inheritance of Aaron was for his posterity. May he impart wisdom to your heart," etc.
He made. Greek, "glory during their age."

The sacred writer pours forth this prayer for God's ministers, (Calmet) that their good things and glory may never end, as long as the Jewish dispensation should last. He is equally solicitous for the priesthood as for the sceptre of David. In both the families of Aaron and of David children succeeded to their father's dignity, and God had made a covenant for that purpose.
David assisted the priests, and greatly advanced God's service, 1 Paralipomenon 23.
His eulogy is given [in] Ecclesiasticus 47.; and he is here only mentioned (Calmet) as a comparison.

46:1.text: Jesus, the son of Nave. So Josue is named in the Greek Bibles. For Josue and Jesus signify the same thing, viz., a Saviour; (Challoner) and Nave is the Greek pronunciation of Nun; (Haydock) or this is an old fault of transcribers.
He was endued with the prophetic spirit, and was raised up by God as a noble figure of the great prophet, Deuteronomy 18:15.
He succeeded Moses in the temporal power; the spiritual belonged to the sons of Aaron, Numbers xxvii.

46:2.text: Israel. He led the people into Chanaan, and divided it among them.

46:3.text: Hands. To pray, (Vatable) or fight, (Bossuet) or to give a signal, Josue 8:26.

46:4.text: The. Greek, "he conducted the wars of the Lord."

46:5.text: In. Greek, "by his hand," (Haydock) and order, so that the day-light continued 24 hours.

46:7.text: Adversaries. The hail destroyed more than the sword, Josue 10:11.

46:8.text: Power. Greek, "complete armour for his warfare was before the Lord, since he," etc. They found it was impossible to resist such weapons.
One. "Follow God," was a maxim of the ancient sages, which they even attributed to Apollo. Josue and Caleb did so in opposition to the rebels, Numbers 14:24.
The rest all perished, (Worthington) if we except those who were not 20 years old, or who did not join in the murmurs of the seditious.

46:9.text: Mercy, and piety, (Calmet) endeavouring to prevent the murmurs of the people, which would draw upon them destruction, as the event shewed.

46:11.text: Land. Hebron, when he was 85 years old, Josue 14:6.

46:13.text: Lord. Abimelec must be excepted, (Calmet) as he was only an usurper.
The Judges were all saved, though some have been great sinners.

46:14.text: Place. At the resurrection, which the Jews firmly believed, Ezechiel 37:3.

46:16.text: People. Though he disapproved of their resolution, 1 Kings 8:6., and Osee 13:11.

46:17.text: And the. Sixtus V, etc., read, "and he beheld the God," 1 Kings 3:7.
Vulgate may signify also, "God beheld Jacob," (Greek) with a favourable eye, and enabled Samuel to restore their fallen state, and to recover the ark.

46:18.text: Because. Greek has not faithful, or what follows.

Yet Grabe's copy retains pistos oraseos, "faithful in seeing" into futurity; so that his predictions were always verified, and he acquired the confidence of all, 1 Kings 3:20. Prophets were formerly styled seers: yet they could not behold God, as he inhabits light inaccessible, 1 Timothy 6:16.
Light. St. James 1:17. seems to allude to this passage.

46:19.text: Without. Greek, "fed with milk," 1 Kings 7:6. The Philistines were discomfited, and continued peaceable during the rest of Samuel's government.

46:21.text: Tyrians, who were auxiliaries, or in league with the Philistines.

46:22.text: Shoe. So Abraham declared he would not receive the latchet of a shoe from the king of Sodom, Genesis 14:23.
Him. All confessed his integrity, before he delivered up the sovereign power to Saul, 1 Kings xii.

46:23.text: Nation. Foretelling that Israel should be defeated. Some doubt whether the soul of Samuel appeared; but it seems clear from this passage, (1 Kings 28:18.; Calmet, Diss.) as he could not otherwise be praised for it.

47:1.text: Nathan. Many other prophets lived at the same time. But he was most conspicuous, and crowned Solomon.
Greek, "and after him Nathan arose to prophesy," (Haydock) and keep up the succession.

47:2.text: Flesh. Greek, "peace-offering." The fat was deemed most delicate.

47:3.text: Played. Tearing them to pieces with the greatest ease, 1 Kings 17:34.

47:6.text: Nation. Who were no longer under the control of the Philistines.

47:7.text: Thousand. This was sung in the cities, (1 Kings 18:7.; Calmet) and excited the envy of Saul, who nevertheless could not prevent David's exaltation.
Did he, or the people who praised the Lord and David.

47:8.text: For ever, is not in Greek. David defeated the Philistines at the beginning of his reign.

47:9.text: His pure and sincere heart pleased God more than his other works.

47:11.text: Altar. Of holocausts. Moses had not appointed singing.

47:12.text: Of his. Greek, "while they praised his holy name, and sounded forth holiness early."
David strove to give all perfection to the sacred ceremonies of religion, that the people might be attracted to the tabernacle, (Calmet) and serve the Lord with gladness.

47:13.text: Sins. Remitting the punishment, (2 Kings 12:13.; Calmet) and the guilt also, (Haydock) in consequence of his repentance.
Covenant. Engaging that his posterity should enjoy the throne (2 Kings 7:12.; Calmet) if they continued faithful, Psalm 131:11., and supra [Ecclesiasticus] Ecclesiasticus 45:31.

47:14.text: Enemies. The piety of David was thus rewarded.
It must have been the greatest satisfaction to him, to see so wise a son on the throne, and all in perfect peace, 3 Kings 1:48., and 4:24.
These blessings were a reward of his virtue.

47:15.text: Youth. When other are less informed. This serves to place the apostacy of Solomon in a more striking light.
The author seems astonished both at the wisdom and at the folly of this prince, (Haydock) and apostrophises him.

47:16.text: Earth. Or explored all the secrets of nature, retexit, 3 Kings 4:29.

47:18.text: Interpretations of riddles. Hence people praised the Lord, 3 Kings 10:24.

47:21.src: St. Augustine, de Gen. ad lit. 11:42.
47:21.text: Subjection. Literally, "thou hadst power over thy body," (Haydock) using it as if it belonged not to God. He was guilty of excess in marrying a thousand wives, and yielding to idolatry, (Calmet) fearing to displease those whom he loved to his own ruin."

47:22.text: Defiled. Consecrating some even to Moloch, (Pineda 7:11.) or having children by those whom the law prohibited, Deuteronomy 7:2., and 23:2.
His sins were punished: yet God's mercy preserved his posterity.

47:23.text: Jeroboam was at the head of it, 3 Kings 12:14.

47:24.text: Lord. David's piety caused the sceptre to be still preserved in his family, (Haydock) though Solomon's conduct deserved to lose it.

47:25.text: Of the. Greek, "a root from him," to govern a part, while Jeroboam reigned over the other tribes of Jacob.
God fulfilled his promises to Jacob and David.

47:26.text: Fathers. Some would hence infer, that Solomon died penitent.

47:27.text: Folly. A most imprudent prince, despised by the whole nation. He answered the people roughly, and lost the ten tribes; but idolatry was his greatest folly, and from this he was never reclaimed, 3 Kings 14:22.

47:29.text: Jeroboam. Though not of his seed, as we only find Roboam mentioned of all the thousand wives of Solomon. He left behind him Jeroboam, notwithstanding his desire to have him slain; and this man brought ruin on the nation.
Sin. This is the usual title given to this introducer of idolatry. He engaged the ten tribes in the worship of the golden calves, which ruined the state and religion of Israel.

47:31.text: Vengeance, (defensio) and put, etc., in not in Greek. Theglatphalasar and Salmanasar carried the tribes away into captivity, 1 Paralipomenon 5:26., and 4 Kings xiv., and 17:6.
Many hereupon opened their eyes, (Menochius) as we may hope. Those who returned with the rest of the Jews relapsed not, at least into idolatry, in such an open manner.

48:1.text: Torch. He was animated with a burning zeal, (Menochius) like John the Baptist, admonishing kings, etc., John 5:35., and Luke 1:17.

48:2.text: And they. Greek, "and by his zeal he diminished them. By," etc.
He slew the priests of Baal, and many perished by famine, 4 Kings xviii.

48:3.text: Heaven. That it should not rain for three years and a-half, 3 Kings 17:1.
Thrice. First upon his sacrifice, and twice to destroy fifty soldiers, Ibid.[3 Kings xvii.] 38., and 4 Kings 1:10.

48:5.text: Man. The son of the widow of Sarepta.

48:6.text: Destruction. The prophets are said to perform what they foretell; to indicate the certainty of the event. Elias denounced death to Achab, Jezabel, Ochozias, and the two Jorams, 4 Kings 9:12., and 2 Paralipomenon 21:12., etc.
Brokest. Greek, "those in honour from," etc., alluding to (Haydock) Ochozias and Joram, 4 Kings 1:16., and 1 Paralipomenon 21:15.

48:7.text: Judgment. Greek, "the reprimand of the Lord," for betraying fear, (3 Kings 19:3, 9, 12.; Haydock) or the reprimand regarded Israel.

48:8.text: Penance. Greek, "reward," (Haydock) or punish the guilty. Hazael and Jehu were anointed by Eliseus, though by order of Elias.
Thee. Eliseus was called from the plough, and adhered to his master, who governed the sons of the prophets in large communities.

48:10.text: Jacob. Protestants reject the authority of this book, partly (Haydock) on account of this (Worthington) ancient tradition. Enoch and Elias will appear in the days of antichrist, to convert many. The Jews will then agree with the Patriarchs, and embrace the religion of Christ.
Elias came in the person of John the Baptist, at the first coming of our Saviour, but he will come in person before his second appearance.

The application which Christ makes of the prediction of Malachy (Malachias 4:6.) to John the Baptist, does not exclude this more distant accomplishment. Elias is still alive in some part of the world, as well as Enoch, whom St. Augustine styles "the first-born (primogenitor) of our resurrection."

48:11.text: Friendship. Like Eliseus, or the disciples of both.
Greek, "who slept in love, for we also shall live indeed; (13.) Elias," etc.
When we shall witness thy return, if we prove faithful, we may expect an immortal name, and not otherwise.
He speaks in the person of those who shall live at that time.

48:13.text: Eliseus. Who received his double spirit, with his garment, 4 Kings 2:15.

48:14.text: Overcome. Or "escape." (Greek; Syriac) He knew what Giezi and the Syrians were doing, at a distance, 4 Kings 5:26., and 6:12.
Prophesied. Or raised the dead to life, 4 Kings 13:21.
On his death-bed (ver. 15.) he also foretold the victory of Joas, ibid. 14.[4 Kings 13:14.?]
The miracle wrought by his relics (Haydock) shewed that he was a true prophet.

48:19.text: Thereof. From Gehon, a fountain on the west, 2 Paralipomenon 32:30.

48:20.text: Lifted. Greek, "removed from Lachis, and, "etc., 4 Kings xviii., etc.

48:23.text: He was. Greek, "and he purified (Haydock; or redeemed, Calmet) them by the hand (ministry) of Isaias. (24.) He," etc.
Prayer prevailed when troops were insufficient, 4 Kings xix.

48:25.text: Sight. Greek, "vision." All his predictions were verified.

48:26.text: Life. Fifteen years, Isaias xxxviii., and 4 Kings 40:11.

48:27.text: At last. After the captivity, (Isaias 2:2.; Calmet) or in the days of the Messias.

49:1.text: Perfumer. The Scripture reproaches this king alone with no fault.

49:3.text: Directed. Greek, "prosperous in converting the people," 4 Kings xxii., and 2 Paralipomenon xxxiv.
He began when he was only eight years old: but set to work more effectually ten years afterwards. The prophets complain of the disorders which prevailed before that period.

49:4.text: Sinners. Or "sins," when iniquity was become fashionable. He consulted no false policy, (Haydock) but boldly exerted himself in God's service, and even invited the Israelites out of his dominions to come and celebrate the passover, 2 Paralipomenon 34:6.
They belonged, in effect to him, as the twelve tribes were all promised to David's family, and the kingdom of Jeroboam was only a rebellion, Ecclesiasticus 47:23.

49:5.text: Sin. The two former effaced their sins by repentance, and never tolerated idolatry, which even Josaphat and Asa did, (3 Kings 22:43., etc.; Calmet) not destroying the high places. Many others yielded not to idol worship.

49:6.text: Despised. Greek, "they failed," and lost the crown.

49:7.text: They gave. Achaz called in to his assistance the king of Assyria, who afterwards claimed the kingdom as tributary to him, 4 Kings 18:7, 14. Greek, "He (God, Calmet; Alexandrian copy has They) gave their horn," etc.

49:8.text: Holiness. Jerusalem, which was styled holy, in the sicles of Simon.
Desolate. No one came to the festivals, Lamentations 1:4.

49:9.text: Renew. To foretell the destruction and establishment of kingdoms, Jeremias 1:5, 10.

49:11.text: Rain. Denoting great misery, Ezechiel 13:11., and 38:22.
And of. Literally, "to do good," (Haydock) or to announce the liberation of the penitent captives, Ezechiel 18:21., and 23:16., etc.

49:12.text: Place. Ecclesiasticus 45:15. The Pharisees adorned the tombs of the prophets, Matthew 23:29. Daniel is not mentioned among them, because he lived at court.

49:13.text: Hand. Most precious, Jeremias 22:24., and Canticle of Canticles 8:6.

49:14.text: Josedec. He was a noble figure of Christ, and helped to build the temple, Aggeus 1:2., and 2:3., and Zacharias 3:1., and 6:11.

49:15.text: Houses. He laboured much for the people's welfare, 2 Esdras v.

49:16.text: He stood up for the Lord, amid the most corrupt people, and was translated, Ecclesiasticus 44:16. He and Joseph have many advantages over the rest.

49:17.text: Joseph. He has not been named, but designated, Ecclesiasticus 44:27.

49:18.text: They prophesied. That is, by their being carried out of Egypt, they verified the prophetic prediction of Joseph, Genesis L.
This sentence is omitted in Greek.
Joseph had intimated that the Israelites should leave Egypt.

49:19.text: Men. The former was the father of the faithful, before the deluge; the latter after.
Beginning. Greek, "in the creation" was appointed to rule.
He alone among men was innocent, coming from the hands of God, and he might glory that he had no other father.

50:1.text: Simon I was "styled the just, on account of his piety towards God, and his good-will to his countrymen." (Josephus, [Jewish Antiquities] 12:2.) Many apply what follows to him.
But Simon II is more probably meant, who opposed the entrance of Philopator into the sanctuary, after he had offered sacrifice for him, in the pompous manner here described.
Temple. Which began to want repairs.
The first lived in the time of Ptolemy I of Egypt, when this book was written; and dies before it was translated, under Ptolemy III, about three hundred years before Christ.

50:2.text: Walls. Greek analemma, "the repairs (or elevated building, Mello. 2 Paralipomenon 22:5.) round the temple," including the court of the Gentiles, which had not been finished, Ezechiel 40:5. It was necessary to build at the edge of the mountain, (Calmet) and the wall was three hundred cubits from the bottom. This was more than double the height of the temple, as it was only one hundred and twenty cubits high.

50:3.text: Out. Of the reservoirs. But he repaired them.

50:4.text: Destruction. When it was in the most imminent danger from Philopater.

50:5.text: Conversation. Greek, "when in the midst of the people, coming out of the house of the veil. (6.) He," etc.
Leaving the most holy place, he was surrounded by crowds of sacred ministers, (ver. 14.) or rather on the day of expiation, he came into the court of the people, to purify them.

50:6.text: Cloud. He was a far superior to the other priests in majestic appearance and attire, as Lucifer is to other stars, etc., ver. 11.

50:7.text: So did. Greek, "on the temple of the Most High," with dazzling effulgence.

50:11.text: Power. That is, with all his vestments, denoting his dignity and authority, (Challoner) and extremely rich, as virtus often implies, Ecclesiasticus 44:3.

50:12.text: His splendid attire of gold and jewels, cast a dazzling light all around.
He was pleased to offer the peace-offering for the king of Egypt, (Bossuet) to honour him, though another priest might have done it.

50:14.text: Palm. A forest of young ones shoots up from its roots; (Job 29:18.) so the high priest shone among the other children of Aaron.

50:15.text: He presented an unblemished victim, with all due solemnity, Leviticus 3:1.

50:16.text: Grape. Pouring wine upon the fire, Exodus 29:40.

50:17.text: The whole sacrifice was pleasing to him.

50:18.text: God. Greek, "the Highest." He had required these sacrifices, as memorials of his covenant, and sovereign dominion, Leviticus 23:34.

50:20.text: House. Greek (Complutensian) and Grabe, "sound, sweet melody was made."

50:21.text: The people prayed for king Ptolemy, to whom they were subject; though the high priest possessed almost sovereign power. The Egyptian monarch was present, (Calmet) and at the end of the sacrifice expressed his resolution to go into the inmost recesses of the temple, from which the people endeavoured to dissuade him. Finding their efforts to be in vain, "they all (addressing themselves to God) said with one accord." (3 Machabees) Grabe substitutes, "wherefore the high priest, Simon, kneeling down before the temple, and stretching forth his hand with solemnity, made this prayer."

50:23.text: Prayer. Greek, "adoration, that they might receive a blessing from the Most High."

Power. In keeping Philopator out of the temple. God granted his request, (Calmet. litaneios) "and scourged him who was so insolent and bold...throwing him like a reed, unable to move, and speechless on the pavement," 2 Machabees 2:26. After Philopator's guards had removed him, the high priest congratulated the people, (ver. 26.) expressing his abhorrence of their enemies in general, though he only mentions three neighbouring nations which had shewn a particular enmity to the Jews, when a contrary behaviour might have been expected, ver. 28.

50:24.text: Now. At the sight of these wonders, the author exhorts the people to be grateful, and full of hope.

50:26.text: His, Simon's, (Menochius) or God's days. When he may judge it convenient.

50:27.text: Abhorreth. Viz., with a holy indignation as enemies of God and persecutors of his people. Such were then the Edomites, who abode in Mount Seir, the Philistines, and the Samaritans, who dwelt in Sichem, and had their schismatical temple in that neighbourhood.
This was the source of continual dissensions.

The Jews and Samaritans are still at variance. The former, in the days of Esdras, pronounced a curse against the latter, forbidding any of them to become converts to their religion, (Grotius) which, if true, shews a very blameable malice. See Deuteronomy 27:4.

Which, etc., is omitted in Greek. These three nations had evinced the greatest hatred towards the Jews, and thus deserved to be accounted objects of horror, though if the author harboured any ill-will, his conduct is not praised, (Calmet) but only recorded.
The Samaritans consisted of Assyrians, Jews, etc., and therefore are styled no nation; and they were foolish in mixing idolatry with the true worship.

50:28.text: Seir. Genesis 14:6. All the Greek copies read corruptly "of Samaria."
Foolish. Thus Christ said, you adore what you know not, John 4:22. (Calmet) See 4 Kings 17:29.

50:29.text: Jesus. He declares his name, and concludes with wishing peace to the observers of these maxims. Grotius thinks that this was inserted by the grandson, (Calmet) who translated the work into Greek.

But there is no reason for this supposition. Solomon puts his name at the beginning and at the end of Ecclesiastes, and he frequently commends his own instructions, as this author does, Ecclesiasticus 33:17,. and 39:16., and 51:19.

50:31.text: Steps. He...shall have the light of life, John 8:12.
Some Greek copies add, "and to the pious he has given wisdom. Blessed be the Lord for ever. So be it. So be it."

51:1.text: Sirach. This prayer may be in imitation of the book of Wisdom.

Grotius assigns it to the grandson, without reason; as the author continues to speak of his travels, etc. He seems to have lived under Onias III and under Seleucus and Antiochus Epiphanes, of Syria, to whom he was probably accused, and hence took occasion to retire into Egypt, where Ptolemy Epiphanes was king.
Others think (Haydock) that he was accused before Ptolemy Lagus, when he took Jerusalem.
We know too little of his life to decide any thing. (Calmet) See Ecclesiasticus 50:1.
Saviour. When we are not able to make a return, we must shew greater marks of gratitude, and particularly to God, for the favours which we have not deserved.

51:5.text: He exaggerates the danger to which he was exposed, as he was not miraculously delivered from the flames, etc., (Calmet) though this might be the case. At least he thanks God for preserving him (Haydock) from the various torments which were then in use.

51:6.text: In. Greek, "from the midst of a fire which I did not enkindle," or deserve by any crime.

51:7.text: Hell. The grave. I was preserved like Jonas.
Unjust. Greek, "the accusation was made to the king, by an unjust tongue. My soul approached unto death, and my life," etc.

51:11.text: World. Shewn to Noe[Noah], Joseph, etc.

51:13.text: Thou. Greek, "I raised my supplicating voice from the earth, and I," etc.

51:14.text: The Father. Grotius asserts boldly, that it should be, "my Father," and that some Christian has changed this. But his authority may be rejected, when he brings no proof; and Bossuet has solidly refuted him. The passage clearly proves the belief of the second person, and seems to allude to Psalm 109:1.

51:18.text: When. He now gives some account of himself, and exhorts all to seek for wisdom.
He was advanced in years, when he fell into the aforesaid danger, after his travels, Ecclesiasticus 34:9.
Wisdom is most easily acquired, while a person is innocent, Ecclesiastes xii.

51:19.text: After her. Being convinced that prayer is requisite for the preservation and improvement of such a treasure. See 3 Kings 3:8.
And. Greek, "from the flower as of a ripening grape. My heart," etc.

51:25.text: Wrestled. Against pleasure, idleness, etc. This shews how arduous a task it is to attain wisdom.

51:27.text: Knowledge. Greek (Alexandrian), "time of harvest." Grabe substitutes, "in purification," (Haydock) or the practice of virtue, Wisdom 1:4.

51:28.text: Heart. Or understanding, as the Hebrew place this in the heart, 3 Kings 3:9.

51:29.text: Her. I did it, with all solicitude, Canticle of Canticles 5:4., and Jeremias 4:19.

51:30.text: Tongue. Eloquence, (Menochius) which I will exert in praising him.

51:31.text: He addresses those who are walking in the broad road.

51:33.text: Silver. Only good-will is necessary, which all may have, Isaias 55:1., Romans 10:6., and Wisdom 6:13.

51:36.text: Gold. The wise man has more opportunities of enriching himself than others; but he despises unnecessary wealth.
We must labour for wisdom more than for riches, and we shall be rewarded hereafter.

51:38.text: The time. It is never too soon to be wise. In doing good let us not fail, (Galatians 6:9.) in order to obtain that food, which remains for ever, Isaias 6:7. Fear God, and keep his commandments, for this is all man, Ecclesiastes 12:13.
This is the sum of the sapiential works, and of all the Scriptures: To depart from evil is understanding, Job 28:28.
